source.title: Contract termination: billing cessation & cost allocation playbook
source.author: Aisha Raman
source.content:
Summary:
This playbook defines standardized procedures for stopping invoicing, allocating final costs, issuing credits or refunds, and updating forecasts and revenue recognition after contract termination. It covers hybrid contracts (hosted + dedicated + private), pre-paid commitments, and purchase-order disputes that impact billing cutoffs. The goal is to minimize revenue leakage, preserve audit evidence, and give clear operational ownership for the termination window.

Scope:
- Applies to all customer contracts (hosted API, Redwood Dedicated, Redwood Private) that enter contract termination, expiration, or early-termination workflows.
- Covers billing cessation, final invoice generation, post-termination credits/refunds, accounting journal guidance, forecast adjustments, and procurement handoffs.
- Does NOT replace legal termination clauses; legal must be consulted for contract-specific language (see Roles & escalation).

Definitions:
- Termination effective date (TED): the date on which service entitlement ends per contract or by mutual agreement.
- Billing cut-off date (BCD): last day for usage to be billed on final invoice (usually TED or TED + reconciliation window).
- Reconciliation window: period after TED for usage reconciliation and dispute (default 14 calendar days).
- Committed capacity: reserved units (Dedicated) for which customer was billed in advance or on commitment schedule.
- Final settlement: the aggregated set of invoices, credits, journal adjustments, and recognition entry required to close the contract.

Principles and guardrails:
1) Customer-facing clarity first: communicate TED, BCD, and expected timeline for credits within 3 business days of termination acknowledgement.
2) Minimize surprise: default to prorated credits for partially unused pre-paid commitments unless contract language dictates otherwise.
3) Preserve audit trail: all adjustments must reference the termination ticket, the contract ID, and include exported usage + capacity logs.
4) Separate operational vs. accounting actions: SRE/Platform performs capacity deprovisioning; Finance records the accounting impacts.

Owners and responsibilities (matrix):
- Contracts / Legal: validate termination clause, confirm financial remedies, approve early-termination fees.
- Finance (Billing & RevRec): calculate credits/refunds, create final invoice(s), post journal entries, update forecasts & revenue schedules.
- Sales / AM: communicate with customer, negotiate final terms, obtain signed termination addendum when required.
- Customer Success / Onboarding: confirm customer data export and operational offboarding checklist.
- Eng-SRE / Platform Ops: schedule capacity reclamation and generate usage snapshots for reconciliation.

End-to-end termination workflow (recommended default):
1) Termination request received (source: Sales/Customer/Legal) -> create Termination case in Zendesk + assign contract ID. (owner: Sales/AM)
2) Legal review of contract clauses and termination liabilities within 3 business days. (owner: Contracts/Legal)
3) Confirm Termination Effective Date (TED) and Billing Cut-off Date (BCD). Default: BCD = TED unless customer requests recon window. Document in case notes and email to customer within 3 business days. (owner: Sales/Finance)
4) Platform generates usage and KV-cache/log snapshots for 30 days prior to TED and for the reconciliation window. (owner: Eng-SRE)
5) Finance calculates final usage invoice, any prorated pre-paid credits, and early termination fees per contract. Initial calculation due within 7 business days after TED. (owner: Finance)
6) Issue final invoice and proposed credit memo to customer. Start reconciliation window (default 14 calendar days) for disputes. (owner: Finance/Sales)
7) If dispute raised, capture evidence (logs, timestamps, customer reports). Mediate between Sales/Eng and customer, escalate to Legal if unresolved within 10 business days. (owner: Sales/Legal)
8) Post-agreement: apply credits/refunds, post revenue recognition adjustments, and close the contract in CRM and billing systems. (owner: Finance)
9) Update forecasts: adjust ARR, committed capacity schedules, and cashflow projections in the FP&A model. (owner: Finance / FP&A)
10) Operational close: deprovision capacity (Dedicated pools) and archive artifacts. (owner: Eng-SRE)

Timelines and SLA table (default):
- Legal review: 3 business days
- Usage snapshot generation: 2 business days after TED
- Initial final calc and draft invoice: 7 business days after TED
- Reconciliation window for disputes: 14 calendar days
- Dispute resolution escalation: escalate to Legal if unresolved after 10 business days of dispute
- Final accounting posted: 20 business days after TED (target)

Thresholds and special handling:
- Large commitments (>= $250,000 ARR equivalent): require executive approval for any non-standard credit or early-termination concession. (owner: Finance + CRO)
- Complex hybrid contracts (mix of hosted + dedicated): require technical usage reconciliation report (SRE output) + confirmed capacity utilization report before credits.
- Pending litigation or regulatory holds: billing changes require Legal + Security sign-off; treat as restricted until cleared.

Accounting guidance (examples):
- Scenario A: Customer with remaining 2 months pre-paid Dedicated capacity (non-refundable per contract) but contract allows pro rata credits.
  - Calculate unused committed period value = pre-paid amount * unused_days / total_days
  - Credit memo issued to customer, debit Deferred Revenue, credit Accounts Payable (refund) or apply credit to final invoice.

- Example journal entries (illustrative):
  1) To remove deferred revenue for unused period:
     DR Deferred Revenue: $40,000
     CR Revenue (or Credit Memo Reserve): $40,000
  2) To issue refund to customer (if refund agreed):
     DR Credit Memo Reserve: $40,000
     CR Cash/Bank: $40,000
  3) If applying credit to outstanding invoice instead of refund:
     DR Credit Memo Reserve: $40,000
     CR Accounts Receivable: $40,000

Note: exact ledger accounts depend on local GAAP; Finance must map to general ledger codes and retain working papers.

Forecasting and FP&A adjustments:
- Immediately move terminated ARR out of committed ARR bucket and into churn/termination schedule for the month of TED.
- Remove expected committed capacity consumption from monthly forecasting model; if there is uncertainty about early termination fee realization, model both a best case (collect fee) and a conservative case (no fee).
- RevRec: recognize only earned revenue; reverse any deferred revenue attributable to the unused service period. Document the reversal with references to contract and termination case.

Procurement and PO handling:
- If termination is due to supplier-side cause (e.g., vendor outage), coordinate procurement to withhold final payments to suppliers until settlement is finalized.
- For customer-issued POs: ensure PO is referenced on final invoices. If PO is retracted post-termination, capture the retraction evidence and follow dispute flow.

Dispute resolution checklist (evidence & artifacts to collect):
- Contract snapshot (final signed agreement) and termination clause cited
- TED and BCD confirmation email chain
- Usage reports and SRE snapshots (including timestamps and tokens/requests)
- Capacity allocation logs for Dedicated pools (UUIDs, allocation windows)
- Billing system export (invoice drafts, tax calculations)
- Customer communications documented in CRM/ticketing

Operational runbook snippets (commands & exports):
- Export usage by customer ID for range:
  > ./infra-tools/export_usage --customer <CUST_ID> --from YYYY-MM-DD --to YYYY-MM-DD --format csv
- Snapshot Dedicated pool allocations:
  > ./capacity-cli list-allocations --pool <POOL_ID> --customer <CUST_ID> --since YYYY-MM-DD --output json
- Create billing export for Finance:
  > ./billing-cli create-final-export --customer <CUST_ID> --bcd YYYY-MM-DD --output /tmp/final-export-<CUST_ID>.csv

Escalation path and SLAs:
- Day 0-3: Sales/Contracts coordinate TED and communication (no escalation).
- Day 4-14: Finance and SRE prepare final instruments. If dispute arises and cannot be resolved in 10 business days -> escalate to Head of Finance + Head of Legal.
- Any judgement requiring write-offs > $100k or systemic billing error -> notify CFO and Chief Revenue Officer within 24 hours.

Examples (common scenarios):
1) Early termination with agreed one-time termination fee: compute fee, net against any credit; if net payable to customer, issue credit memo.
2) Expiration of committed capacity: automatically trigger final invoice run at BCD; if customer auto-renews not in effect, mark commitment as expired and deallocate capacity after reconciliation window.
3) Mixed model: customer used hosted API past TED due to delayed deprovisioning — include prorated usage on final invoice and document deprovision delay as incident for SRE follow-up.

Postmortem and continuous improvement:
- For any contract termination that results in a non-standard credit or operational failure, run a 30-day postmortem (owner: Finance + Eng-SRE + Sales). Document root cause and update this playbook if a repeat pattern emerges.

FAQs (short):
- Q: When do we automatically refund versus apply credits?
  A: Default is to apply credit to outstanding balance; refunds require signoff if cash > $10k.
- Q: Who approves write-offs?
  A: Finance approves < $25k; Director Finance approves $25k-$100k; CFO approves > $100k.

Appendix: template email to customer (use CRM merge tokens):
Subject: Confirmation of contract termination for {{contract_id}}
Body:
Hi {{customer_name}},

This confirms the termination of contract {{contract_id}} effective {{TED}}. Your final billing cut-off date is {{BCD}}. We will prepare a final invoice and any applicable credits within 7 business days. A reconciliation window of 14 days will be open for disputes. Please reply to this thread to confirm receipt.

Owner contacts:
- Billing (Finance): billing@redwood.example.com
- Contracts (Legal): contracts@redwood.example.com
- Platform Ops: platform-ops@redwood.example.com

Revision history:
- 2026-11-02: Created by Aisha Raman
- 2026-12-15: Added dispute checklist and sample journal entries (reviewers: Diego Morales)
- 2027-01-12: Clarified timelines for large commitments and added escalation thresholds

source.title: Capacity cost hedging and billing coordination guide
source.author: Priya Nair
source.body:
Overview:

This guide describes Redwood Inference's standardized approach for aligning capacity purchasing (GPU/pool reservations and cloud commit tiers), short‑term hedging strategies, and billing/revenue-recognition coordination. The goal is to reduce P&L volatility from usage seasonality and to ensure procurement, legal, finance, and product teams share a synchronized playbook for commits, overage handling, and invoice timing.

Scope:
- Applies to Hosted API and Dedicated capacity products; Private deployments follow a separate procurement cadence but should reference the hedging principles below.
- Covers forecasting windows of 1 month, 3 months, and 12 months.
- Includes guidance for monthly usage commits, reserved GPU capacity, spot/burst usage policies, and contract features that materially affect revrec (e.g., non-refundable prepayments, renewal triggers).

Key definitions:
- Baseline Commitment: recurring reserved capacity (monthly or annual) with a fixed price and term.
- Hedge Reserve: an incremental short-term capacity reservation (1–3 months) used to smooth cost for anticipated spikes.
- Overage Pool: metered usage billed at overage rates when actual consumption exceeds committed capacity.
- True‑up window: the period after which usage is reconciled and credits/debits applied.

Principles:
1) Visibility and single source of truth: Finance owns the committed-capacity ledger in the finance data warehouse; product/infra feed daily usage estimates into it.
2) Risk sharing: Tiered commitments should align incentives—teams with product roadmap-driven demand (e.g., Search team) should internalize part of hedging cost.
3) Minimizing revrec complexity: Prefer prepaid, short-term hedges over long multi-year irregular payments unless contractually necessary.
4) Operational simplicity: Standardize three hedging instruments: monthly hedge blocks, quarterly top-up, and burst credits.

Roles and ownership:
- Finance (Owner): final forecast signoff, commit economics analysis, and revrec guidance.
- Procurement: negotiates vendor terms, PO issuance, and manages supplier credit and invoices.
- Legal: reviews contract clauses that affect recognition (non-refundable, termination penalties, service credits).
- Product/Infra: provides demand signals, seasonality drivers, and validates performance requirements.
- Sales: communicates customer-facing contract terms that interact with capacity (enterprise reserved pools).

Forecasting model (operational):
1) Inputs (daily): API token usage by SKU, median/95th p50/p95 tokens per request, mean session length, number of concurrent sessions.
2) Aggregation (weekly): compute 3-week trailing average and 98th percentile daily peak.
3) Rolling forecast buckets:
   - Short (0–30 days): p95 daily peak scaled by planned launches and promotions.
   - Medium (30–90 days): trailing average plus planned cadence impact (feature flags, marketing).
   - Long (90–365 days): conservative CAGR applied to baseline plus pipeline-sourced enterprise commitments.

Suggested hedging instruments and thresholds:
- Monthly Hedge Block: purchase when short forecast variance > 15% vs baseline for next 30 days. Typical size: 20–50% of expected incremental peak.
- Quarterly Top-up: used if medium forecast projects > 20% sustained increase for 60+ days.
- Burst Credits: vendor-managed spot capacity; use when short, unexpected spikes < 7 days.
- Trigger example: If expected 30‑day additional token volume > 10B tokens (~$xxk cost), open Monthly Hedge approval.

Procurement sync and PO cadence:
1) Weekly cadence: Product/Infra provides a 30-day demand delta to Procurement by Monday 10:00 PT.
2) Procurement evaluation: same-day tiered sourcing (internal reserved pool vs. cloud provider commit) with 24–48 hour turnaround for standard monthly hedges.
3) PO issuance: standard template (see appendix) — include term, price, termination notice (60 days), and true-up window.
4) Invoice routing: route hedging invoices to Finance CC procurement@redwood.ai and attach PO number; flagged hedging invoices are reconciled to the commit ledger within 3 business days.

Contract considerations (legal checklist):
- Payment terms: prefer net-30 for hedges; prepaid monthly hedges allowed with clear revrec note.
- Refundability: Obtain explicit language for refunds or reallocation in case of cancelled product launches.
- Capacity transfer: include clause allowing internal re-assignment of reserved capacity across Redwood-owned projects.
- Termination & penalties: cap termination penalties; define force majeure and migration assistance.
- Usage metrics & reporting: define the metric vendor will report for capacity consumption and the cadence (daily/weekly).

Billing and revenue recognition coordination:
- Hedge accounting approach:
  - Prepaid monthly hedges are treated as deferred revenue and recognized as monthly cost of goods sold (COGS) as capacity is consumed or as time‑based amortization if capacity is fungible and time-bound.
  - Non-refundable upfront multi-month hedges follow the revenue recognition policy: amortize over contractual service period unless a delivered performance obligation exists earlier.
- Invoicing timing: Finance must match hedge invoices to commits within the same month; mis-matched items are placed on a 1-cycle exception report for review.
- Example: $120k prepaid hedge for 3 months → recognize $40k/month as cost and amortize deferred revenue accordingly unless usage allocation requires different treatment.

Operational playbook: step-by-step (example: 30-day spike mitigation)
1) Trigger: Product alerts 7-day rolling usage > 130% of baseline for two consecutive days.
2) Action: Product files Demand Delta ticket and tags procurement + finance with forecast delta and required start date.
3) Procurement: evaluates Monthly Hedge Block vs burst credits; if hedge chosen, draft PO and route for approval.
4) Finance: performs quick economic check (IRR impact, revrec implications) and approves up to the threshold ($50k) or escalates otherwise.
5) Execution: procurement issues PO, vendor confirms, invoice routed, and capacity is allocated.
6) Post-mortem: within 10 business days after spike, Product and Finance co-author a short RCA and cost allocation note.

Approval thresholds (examples):
- Auto-approve (Procurement + Finance): hedges <= $50,000 and duration <= 3 months.
- Director-level approval: $50,001–$250,000 or <12 month term.
- VP/CFO approval: > $250,000 or multi-year commitments.

Example calculations:
- Calculation inputs: baseline tokens/day = 5B, expected spike average = 9B tokens/day for 30 days.
- Incremental tokens = (9B - 5B) * 30 = 120B tokens.
- If vendor committed price = $0.0000125 per token reserved, hedge cost ≈ 1,500,000 (120B * 0.0000125).
- Finance will compare the above to expected overage pricing and potential lost revenue from throttling to decide whether to hedge.

Configuration table (recommended defaults):
| Item | Default | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Short-term hedge trigger | +15% 30-day variance | Measured vs baseline commits |
| Hedge minimum size | $5,000 | Avoid micro-POs |
| True-up cadence | Monthly | Reconcile commits to usage |
| Invoice routing SLA | 3 business days | To commit ledger |

Data and tooling snippets:
- Example SQL to surface 30-day delta (internal warehouse):
  SELECT date_trunc('day', ts) as day, sum(tokens) as tokens_per_day
  FROM analytics.api_usage
  WHERE sku IN ('hosted-gen', 'dedicated-gpu')
  AND ts >= current_date - interval '30 days'
  GROUP BY day
  ORDER BY day desc;

- Procurement CLI example (prototype) to open a hedge request: 
  redwood procurement create-hedge --term monthly --start 2026-09-01 --amount 200000 --reason \"Search release Q3\" --owner product-search@example.com

Reporting and KPIs:
- Hedge effectiveness: (avoided overage spend - hedge cost) / hedge cost, measured quarterly. Target > 20% positive contribution.
- Forecast accuracy: mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) for 30-day window; target < 12%.
- Invoice reconciliation SLA: percent reconciled within 3 business days; target 95%.

RCA and continuous improvement:
- After any hedging event > $50k or any false positive hedge (unused capacity > 40%), Product and Finance must produce a 10-slide retrospective: why forecast missed, what signals were available, and recommended model changes.

Appendix A — Standard PO template fields:
- PO title, PO number, vendor legal entity, capacity description (units and pricing), start/end dates, termination notice, refund/reassignment clause, invoice instructions, GL codes.

Appendix B — RevRec references:
- See revenue-recognition/internal-policy-2025 for detailed guidance on amortization vs usage-based recognition.

Appendix C — Escalation contacts:
- Finance: priya.nair@redwood.ai
- Procurement: procurement@redwood.ai
- Legal: contracts@redwood.ai
- Product Ops: product-ops@redwood.ai

Related pages and links:
- Dedicated capacity cost allocation playbook (link internal)
- Contracting governance for capacity commits (link internal)

Document change log:
- 2026-03-15: created by Priya Nair (initial draft)
- 2026-06-02: updated with procurement CLI and KPI targets
- 2026-09-10: published after review by Finance and Procurement

End of document.

title: Capacity cost hedging and billing coordination guide
preview:
Overview:

This guide describes Redwood Inference's standardized approach for aligning capacity purchasing (GPU/pool reservations and cloud commit tiers), short‑term hedging strategies, and billing/revenue-recognition coordination. The goal is to reduce P&L volatility from usage seasonality and to ensure procurement, legal, finance, and product teams share a synchronized playbook for commits, overage handling, and invoice timing.

Scope:
- Applies to Hosted API and Dedicated capacity products; Private deployments follow a separate procurement cadence but should reference the hedging principles below.
- Covers forecasting windows of 1 month, 3 months, and 12 months.
- Includes guidance for monthly usage commits, reserved GPU capacity, spot/burst usage policies, and contract features that materially affect revrec (e.g., non-refundable prepayments, renewal triggers).

Key definitions:
- Baseline Commitment: recurring reserved capacity (monthly or annual) with a fixed price and term.
- Hedge Reserve: an incremental short-term capacity reservation (1–3 months) used to smooth cost for anticipated spikes.
- Overage Pool: metered usage billed at overage rates when actual consumption exceeds committed capacity.
- True‑up window: the period after which usage is reconciled and credits/debits applied.

Principles:
1) Visibility and single source of truth: Finance owns the committed-capacity ledger in the finance data warehouse; product/infra feed daily usage estimates into it.
2) Risk sharing: Tiered commitments should align incentives—teams with product roadmap-driven demand (e.g., Search team) should internalize part of hedging cost.
3) Minimizing revrec complexity: Prefer prepaid, short-term hedges over long multi-year irregular payments unless contractually necessary.
4) Operational simplicity: Standardize three hedging instruments: monthly hedge blocks, quarterly top-up, and burst credits.

Roles and ownership:
- Finance (Owner): final forecast signoff, commit economics analysis, and revrec guidance.
- Procurement: negotiates vendor terms, PO issuance, and manages supplier credit and invoices.
- Legal: reviews contract clauses that affect recognition (non-refundable, termination penalties, service credits).
- Product/Infra: provides demand signals, seasonality drivers, and validates performance requirements.
- Sales: communicates customer-facing contract terms that interact with capacity (enterprise reserved pools).

Forecasting model (operational):
1) Inputs (daily): API token usage by SKU, median/95th p50/p95 tokens per request, mean session length, number of concurrent sessions.
2) Aggregation (weekly): compute 3-week trailing average and 98th percentile daily peak.
3) Rolling forecast buckets:
   - Short (0–30 days): p95 daily peak scaled by planned launches and promotions.
   - Medium (30–90 days): trailing average plus planned cadence impact (feature flags, marketing).
   - Long (90–365 days): conservative CAGR applied to baseline plus pipeline-sourced enterprise commitments.

Suggested hedging instruments and thresholds:
- Monthly Hedge Block: purchase when short forecast variance > 15% vs baseline for next 30 days. Typical size: 20–50% of expected incremental peak.
- Quarterly Top-up: used if medium forecast projects > 20% sustained increase for 60+ days.
- Burst Credits: vendor-managed spot capacity; use when short, unexpected spikes < 7 days.
- Trigger example: If expected 30‑day additional token volume > 10B tokens (~$xxk cost), open Monthly Hedge approval.

Procurement sync and PO cadence:
1) Weekly cadence: Product/Infra provides a 30-day demand delta to Procurement by Monday 10:00 PT.
2) Procurement evaluation: same-day tiered sourcing (internal reserved pool vs. cloud provider commit) with 24–48 hour turnaround for standard monthly hedges.
3) PO issuance: standard template (see appendix) — include term, price, termination notice (60 days), and true-up window.
4) Invoice routing: route hedging invoices to Finance CC procurement@redwood.ai and attach PO number; flagged hedging invoices are reconciled to the commit ledger within 3 business days.

Contract considerations (legal checklist):
- Payment terms: prefer net-30 for hedges; prepaid monthly hedges allowed with clear revrec note.
- Refundability: Obtain explicit language for refunds or reallocation in case of cancelled product launches.
- Capacity transfer: include clause allowing internal re-assignment of reserved capacity across Redwood-owned projects.
- Termination & penalties: cap termination penalties; define force majeure and migration assistance.
- Usage metrics & reporting: define the metric vendor will report for capacity consumption and the cadence (daily/weekly).

Billing and revenue recognition coordination:
- Hedge accounting approach:
  - Prepaid monthly hedges are treated as deferred revenue and recognized as monthly cost of goods sold (COGS) as capacity is consumed or as time‑based amortization if capacity is fungible and time-bound.
  - Non-refundable upfront multi-month hedges follow the revenue recognition policy: amortize over contractual service period unless a delivered performance obligation exists earlier.
- Invoicing timing: Finance must match hedge invoices to commits within the same month; mis-matched items are placed on a 1-cycle exception report for review.
- Example: $120k prepaid hedge for 3 months → recognize $40k/month as cost and amortize deferred revenue accordingly unless usage allocation requires different treatment.

Operational playbook: step-by-step (example: 30-day spike mitigation)
1) Trigger: Product alerts 7-day rolling usage > 130% of baseline for two consecutive days.
2) Action: Product files Demand Delta ticket and tags procurement + finance with forecast delta and required start date.
3) Procurement: evaluates Monthly Hedge Block vs burst credits; if hedge chosen, draft PO and route for approval.
4) Finance: performs quick economic check (IRR impact, revrec implications) and approves up to the threshold ($50k) or escalates otherwise.
5) Execution: procurement issues PO, vendor confirms, invoice routed, and capacity is allocated.
6) Post-mortem: within 10 business days after spike, Product and Finance co-author a short RCA and cost allocation note.

Approval thresholds (examples):
- Auto-approve (Procurement + Finance): hedges <= $50,000 and duration <= 3 months.
- Director-level approval: $50,001–$250,000 or <12 month term.
- VP/CFO approval: > $250,000 or multi-year commitments.

Example calculations:
- Calculation inputs: baseline tokens/day = 5B, expected spike average = 9B tokens/day for 30 days.
- Incremental tokens = (9B - 5B) * 30 = 120B tokens.
- If vendor committed price = $0.0000125 per token reserved, hedge cost ≈ 1,500,000 (120B * 0.0000125).
- Finance will compare the above to expected overage pricing and potential lost revenue from throttling to decide whether to hedge.

Configuration table (recommended defaults):
| Item | Default | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Short-term hedge trigger | +15% 30-day variance | Measured vs baseline commits |
| Hedge minimum size | $5,000 | Avoid micro-POs |
| True-up cadence | Monthly | Reconcile commits to usage |
| Invoice routing SLA | 3 business days | To commit ledger |

Data and tooling snippets:
- Example SQL to surface 30-day delta (internal warehouse):
  SELECT date_trunc('day', ts) as day, sum(tokens) as tokens_per_day
  FROM analytics.api_usage
  WHERE sku IN ('hosted-gen', 'dedicated-gpu')
  AND ts >= current_date - interval '30 days'
  GROUP BY day
  ORDER BY day desc;

- Procurement CLI example (prototype) to open a hedge request: 
  redwood procurement create-hedge --term monthly --start 2026-09-01 --amount 200000 --reason \"Search release Q3\" --owner product-search@example.com

Reporting and KPIs:
- Hedge effectiveness: (avoided overage spend - hedge cost) / hedge cost, measured quarterly. Target > 20% positive contribution.
- Forecast accuracy: mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) for 30-day window; target < 12%.
- Invoice reconciliation SLA: percent reconciled within 3 business days; target 95%.

RCA and continuous improvement:
- After any hedging event > $50k or any false positive hedge (unused capacity > 40%), Product and Finance must produce a 10-slide retrospective: why forecast missed, what signals were available, and recommended model changes.

Appendix A — Standard PO template fields:
- PO title, PO number, vendor legal entity, capacity description (units and pricing), start/end dates, termination notice, refund/reassignment clause, invoice instructions, GL codes.

Appendix B — RevRec references:
- See revenue-recognition/internal-policy-2025 for detailed guidance on amortization vs usage-based recognition.

Appendix C — Escalation contacts:
- Finance: priya.nair@redwood.ai
- Procurement: procurement@redwood.ai
- Legal: contracts@redwood.ai
- Product Ops: product-ops@redwood.ai

Related pages and links:
- Dedicated capacity cost allocation playbook (link internal)
- Contracting governance for capacity commits (link internal)

Document change log:
- 2026-03-15: created by Priya Nair (initial draft)
- 2026-06-02: updated with procurement CLI and KPI targets
- 2026-09-10: published after review by Finance and Procurement

End of document.

source.title: Mitigation: Provisional credit reversal causing autopay hold (RevRec & Billing)
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
## Purpose
This page documents the operational mitigation and finance/legal guidance for incidents where a support-issued provisional credit is automatically reversed during reconciliation, leading to a negative invoice balance and an autopay hold. It consolidates learnings from SUP-121212 (Acme Health) and establishes a repeatable response for RevRec, Billing Engineering, and Customer Support.

## Background (what happened)
In SUP-121212, a provisional credit issued for hourly smoothing was reversed automatically during an overnight/hourly reconciliation run. The reversal produced a negative balance on the customer invoice and triggered an automated autopay hold. For a dedicated customer, the payment-hold state also restricted production API keys and degraded integrations.

### Reference timeline (example case: Acme Health)
- 2026-03-10 22:14 UTC: Support issued provisional credit (REF: PC-4431) for $125.00 USD.
- 2026-03-11 02:07 UTC: Hourly postback/reconciler job ran (run_id=20260311-0207).
- 2026-03-11 02:09 UTC: Accounting audit recorded a provisional credit reversal (reason=duplicate_credit_detected).
- 2026-03-11 03:30 UTC: Autopay hold applied due to negative balance (-$125.00).
- 2026-03-13 09:15 UTC: Manual credit applied to restore positive balance; autopay hold cleared; keys restored.

### Symptoms / signals
- Invoice shows a credit reversal line matching the amount of a recent provisional credit.
- Accounting audit trail contains events similar to: provisional_credit_reversal reason=duplicate_credit_detected.
- Reconciler logs warn about multiple postbacks for the same org/window (e.g., sources include both legacy and v1 identifiers).
- Finance automation applies autopay hold shortly after negative balance is computed.

## Root cause (technical + process)
### Primary technical root cause
A reconciler deduplication rule incorrectly matched postback source aliases and treated a support-issued provisional credit as a duplicate credit, triggering automatic reversal during reconciliation. The issue was exacerbated when both legacy and v1 postback sources were present for the same usage window.

### Contributing factors
- Dedup signature matching was tightened in the dedup rule set and did not account for legacy postback source aliases.
- Provisional credits issued via Support were not explicitly excluded from dedup reversal logic.
- Autopay hold automation acted on negative balances without an emergency override path for time-sensitive customer-impacting situations.

## Current status (as of last_updated)
- Engineering fix tracked under INT-4421 has been deployed to production to normalize/accept legacy postback aliases in signature matching.
- Dedup rule set has been updated to prevent automatic reversal of support-issued provisional credits when the credit is tagged as Support-issued and within an allowed adjustment window.
- Finance automation policy has been updated for dedicated customers to allow a limited “emergency release” process with Billing approval for small amounts when customer availability is impacted.

## Immediate mitigation playbook (when an incident occurs)
### 1) Confirm the condition
1. Identify the most recent provisional credit ID (e.g., PC-####) and amount.
2. Confirm reversal in accounting audit trail and/or invoice lines.
3. Confirm the autopay hold trigger reason is negative_balance (and note the amount).
4. Confirm whether the customer is dedicated and whether keys/endpoints are restricted.

Evidence to collect (attach to the case):
- Invoice ID, org_id, and the credit + reversal line items.
- Reconciler run_id and time window.
- Accounting audit event(s) for credit issuance and reversal.

### 2) Stabilize customer operations
Goal: remove payment-hold restrictions as quickly as possible while preserving auditability.

Approved stabilization actions:
- Apply a manual provisional credit (or adjustment) to restore balance to >= $0.00.
- Request Finance to clear the autopay hold after the balance is corrected.

Constraints:
- If the adjustment is > $100 USD (or local equivalent), Billing must provide written approval in the ticket before Finance executes the override.
- All actions must be recorded in the support case with: actor, timestamp (UTC), amount, rationale, and references to evidence.

### 3) Communicate to the customer (Support-owned)
Minimum customer update should include:
- Confirmation that the account balance was corrected and the autopay hold was cleared.
- High-level explanation: automated reconciliation incorrectly reversed a support-issued provisional credit.
- Assurance: engineering fix in progress or deployed; monitoring added.

Do not share internal rule names/commit hashes with customers. Use non-technical phrasing and keep it factual.

## Permanent remediation (required follow-ups)
### Engineering (Billing/SRE)
- Maintain alias normalization in postback signature matching to prevent false duplicate detection.
- Maintain explicit exclusion logic so support-issued provisional credits are not eligible for automatic dedup reversal.
- Add regression tests covering: legacy + v1 source alias combinations, support-issued credit tagging, and reconciliation edge cases.
- Add monitoring/alerting on accounting audit events matching: provisional_credit_reversal where reason includes duplicate_credit_detected.

### Finance/RevRec
- Ensure reversal/credit adjustments are appropriately classified for revenue recognition and that customer communications do not imply revenue-impacting promises without approval.
- Maintain an emergency override procedure for dedicated customers where availability is impacted.
- Periodically review autopay hold thresholds and automation sensitivity for known false-negative-balance scenarios.

### Support/Customer Success
- Maintain a standard customer response template (see “Customer communication template”).
- For dedicated customers, confirm whether any production restrictions occurred and document the window.

## Policy: Autopay hold emergency release (dedicated customers)
### When to use
- Dedicated customer with production impact (keys restricted, endpoint errors, or integrations degraded), and
- Negative balance was caused by a suspected erroneous reversal of a support-issued provisional credit.

### Approval and controls
- Billing on-call approval required for adjustments > $100 USD.
- Finance executes hold clearance only after balance is restored to non-negative.
- All actions must be logged in the ticket and in the finance adjustment system with cross-references.

### Audit requirements
For each emergency release:
- Ticket link (SUP-####)
- Invoice ID and org_id
- Amount and currency
- Reason code: “ERRONEOUS_REVERSAL_SUSPECTED”
- Approver (name) and timestamp (UTC)

## Customer communication template
Subject: Billing hold cleared; provisional credit reversal investigation

Message:
- We identified that an automated billing process reversed a provisional credit that had been applied to your account.
- We have corrected your balance and cleared the automatic payment hold.
- Our engineering team has implemented (or is implementing) a fix to prevent recurrence and we are monitoring for similar events.
- If you observed any service impact, please share timestamps; we will validate and follow up.

## Appendix A: Example log indicators (internal)
- billing.reconciler: starting reconciliation run (run_id=...)
- billing.reconciler: detected multiple postbacks (sources: postback-v1, postback-legacy)
- accounting.service: provisional_credit_reversal credit_id=PC-#### amount=... reason=duplicate_credit_detected
- finance.automation: autopay_hold applied due_to negative_balance ...

## Appendix B: Links / tracking
- Primary incident example: SUP-121212
- Engineering tracking: INT-4421
- Runbook: Hourly Postback Reconciler Runbook (see related_pages)

## Change log
- 2026-03-14: Initial publication based on SUP-121212.
- 2026-03-18: Updated to reflect production deployment of alias normalization and provisional-credit exclusion behavior.
- 2026-03-22: Added Finance emergency release controls and audit requirements; updated customer template.

title: Mitigation: Provisional credit reversal causing autopay hold (RevRec & Billing)
preview:
## Purpose
This page documents the operational mitigation and finance/legal guidance for incidents where a support-issued provisional credit is automatically reversed during reconciliation, leading to a negative invoice balance and an autopay hold. It consolidates learnings from SUP-121212 (Acme Health) and establishes a repeatable response for RevRec, Billing Engineering, and Customer Support.

## Background (what happened)
In SUP-121212, a provisional credit issued for hourly smoothing was reversed automatically during an overnight/hourly reconciliation run. The reversal produced a negative balance on the customer invoice and triggered an automated autopay hold. For a dedicated customer, the payment-hold state also restricted production API keys and degraded integrations.

### Reference timeline (example case: Acme Health)
- 2026-03-10 22:14 UTC: Support issued provisional credit (REF: PC-4431) for $125.00 USD.
- 2026-03-11 02:07 UTC: Hourly postback/reconciler job ran (run_id=20260311-0207).
- 2026-03-11 02:09 UTC: Accounting audit recorded a provisional credit reversal (reason=duplicate_credit_detected).
- 2026-03-11 03:30 UTC: Autopay hold applied due to negative balance (-$125.00).
- 2026-03-13 09:15 UTC: Manual credit applied to restore positive balance; autopay hold cleared; keys restored.

### Symptoms / signals
- Invoice shows a credit reversal line matching the amount of a recent provisional credit.
- Accounting audit trail contains events similar to: provisional_credit_reversal reason=duplicate_credit_detected.
- Reconciler logs warn about multiple postbacks for the same org/window (e.g., sources include both legacy and v1 identifiers).
- Finance automation applies autopay hold shortly after negative balance is computed.

## Root cause (technical + process)
### Primary technical root cause
A reconciler deduplication rule incorrectly matched postback source aliases and treated a support-issued provisional credit as a duplicate credit, triggering automatic reversal during reconciliation. The issue was exacerbated when both legacy and v1 postback sources were present for the same usage window.

### Contributing factors
- Dedup signature matching was tightened in the dedup rule set and did not account for legacy postback source aliases.
- Provisional credits issued via Support were not explicitly excluded from dedup reversal logic.
- Autopay hold automation acted on negative balances without an emergency override path for time-sensitive customer-impacting situations.

## Current status (as of last_updated)
- Engineering fix tracked under INT-4421 has been deployed to production to normalize/accept legacy postback aliases in signature matching.
- Dedup rule set has been updated to prevent automatic reversal of support-issued provisional credits when the credit is tagged as Support-issued and within an allowed adjustment window.
- Finance automation policy has been updated for dedicated customers to allow a limited “emergency release” process with Billing approval for small amounts when customer availability is impacted.

## Immediate mitigation playbook (when an incident occurs)
### 1) Confirm the condition
1. Identify the most recent provisional credit ID (e.g., PC-####) and amount.
2. Confirm reversal in accounting audit trail and/or invoice lines.
3. Confirm the autopay hold trigger reason is negative_balance (and note the amount).
4. Confirm whether the customer is dedicated and whether keys/endpoints are restricted.

Evidence to collect (attach to the case):
- Invoice ID, org_id, and the credit + reversal line items.
- Reconciler run_id and time window.
- Accounting audit event(s) for credit issuance and reversal.

### 2) Stabilize customer operations
Goal: remove payment-hold restrictions as quickly as possible while preserving auditability.

Approved stabilization actions:
- Apply a manual provisional credit (or adjustment) to restore balance to >= $0.00.
- Request Finance to clear the autopay hold after the balance is corrected.

Constraints:
- If the adjustment is > $100 USD (or local equivalent), Billing must provide written approval in the ticket before Finance executes the override.
- All actions must be recorded in the support case with: actor, timestamp (UTC), amount, rationale, and references to evidence.

### 3) Communicate to the customer (Support-owned)
Minimum customer update should include:
- Confirmation that the account balance was corrected and the autopay hold was cleared.
- High-level explanation: automated reconciliation incorrectly reversed a support-issued provisional credit.
- Assurance: engineering fix in progress or deployed; monitoring added.

Do not share internal rule names/commit hashes with customers. Use non-technical phrasing and keep it factual.

## Permanent remediation (required follow-ups)
### Engineering (Billing/SRE)
- Maintain alias normalization in postback signature matching to prevent false duplicate detection.
- Maintain explicit exclusion logic so support-issued provisional credits are not eligible for automatic dedup reversal.
- Add regression tests covering: legacy + v1 source alias combinations, support-issued credit tagging, and reconciliation edge cases.
- Add monitoring/alerting on accounting audit events matching: provisional_credit_reversal where reason includes duplicate_credit_detected.

### Finance/RevRec
- Ensure reversal/credit adjustments are appropriately classified for revenue recognition and that customer communications do not imply revenue-impacting promises without approval.
- Maintain an emergency override procedure for dedicated customers where availability is impacted.
- Periodically review autopay hold thresholds and automation sensitivity for known false-negative-balance scenarios.

### Support/Customer Success
- Maintain a standard customer response template (see “Customer communication template”).
- For dedicated customers, confirm whether any production restrictions occurred and document the window.

## Policy: Autopay hold emergency release (dedicated customers)
### When to use
- Dedicated customer with production impact (keys restricted, endpoint errors, or integrations degraded), and
- Negative balance was caused by a suspected erroneous reversal of a support-issued provisional credit.

### Approval and controls
- Billing on-call approval required for adjustments > $100 USD.
- Finance executes hold clearance only after balance is restored to non-negative.
- All actions must be logged in the ticket and in the finance adjustment system with cross-references.

### Audit requirements
For each emergency release:
- Ticket link (SUP-####)
- Invoice ID and org_id
- Amount and currency
- Reason code: “ERRONEOUS_REVERSAL_SUSPECTED”
- Approver (name) and timestamp (UTC)

## Customer communication template
Subject: Billing hold cleared; provisional credit reversal investigation

Message:
- We identified that an automated billing process reversed a provisional credit that had been applied to your account.
- We have corrected your balance and cleared the automatic payment hold.
- Our engineering team has implemented (or is implementing) a fix to prevent recurrence and we are monitoring for similar events.
- If you observed any service impact, please share timestamps; we will validate and follow up.

## Appendix A: Example log indicators (internal)
- billing.reconciler: starting reconciliation run (run_id=...)
- billing.reconciler: detected multiple postbacks (sources: postback-v1, postback-legacy)
- accounting.service: provisional_credit_reversal credit_id=PC-#### amount=... reason=duplicate_credit_detected
- finance.automation: autopay_hold applied due_to negative_balance ...

## Appendix B: Links / tracking
- Primary incident example: SUP-121212
- Engineering tracking: INT-4421
- Runbook: Hourly Postback Reconciler Runbook (see related_pages)

## Change log
- 2026-03-14: Initial publication based on SUP-121212.
- 2026-03-18: Updated to reflect production deployment of alias normalization and provisional-credit exclusion behavior.
- 2026-03-22: Added Finance emergency release controls and audit requirements; updated customer template.

source.title: Invoice "Overage Adjustment" After Quota Threshold Change — Proration & Rate-Lookup RCA (2026)
source.author: Jordan Lee
source.body:
## Summary
A dedicated-plan customer (Maple Health) reported an unexpected invoice increase immediately after changing their quota threshold from **1M tokens to 2M tokens** via the Console UI on **2026-03-09**. The subsequent invoice generated on **2026-03-10** included a large line item labeled **"overage adjustment"**.

Engineering confirmed the charge was caused by a **rate-lookup fallback to a legacy/default mapping** during a narrow failure window in the quota metadata write path. The invoicing pipeline proceeded using the fallback rate rather than retrying rate lookup after metadata became available.

Finance issued a manual credit and Engineering shipped a hotfix to prevent recurrence.

---

## Customer & Scope
- Customer: **Maple Health**
- Tier: **Dedicated**
- Environment: **prod (us-east)**
- Affected model(s) observed in ledger context: **gpt-4o-mini**
- Primary artifact: **invoice_82419** (customer-provided PDF referenced in ticket)

### Impact
- Customer received an invoice containing an **"overage adjustment"** line item for **~1.1M tokens** at an unexpectedly high/legacy rate.
- No service downtime was reported; impact was limited to **billing correctness and customer trust**.

### Additional exposure (post-incident audit)
- We identified **7** other accounts with quota updates during the same failure window.
- **0** additional incorrect invoices were ultimately issued (either no invoice generated in the window or invoices were generated after metadata became consistent).

---

## Timeline (UTC)
- **2026-03-09 17:42** — Console quota change event recorded for Maple Health (quota_update by user@maplehealth.com).
- **2026-03-09 (deploy window)** — Invoicing job **v2.3.1** running; quota metadata store experienced intermittent **HTTP 500** responses.
- **2026-03-10** — Invoice generated; includes **"overage adjustment"** line item.
- **2026-03-11** — Support engineering flags known proration edge case: rate/proration lookup can fall back when quota metadata is missing.
- **2026-03-12** — Finance places provisional credit hold pending confirmation.
- **2026-03-15** — Root cause confirmed: quota update event emitted, metadata write failed transiently, invoicing job proceeded with fallback.
- **2026-03-18** — Hotfix deployed: invoicing job **v2.3.2** (retry rate lookup; block fallback on proration-required paths).
- **2026-03-19** — Manual credit issued to Maple Health; customer notified with explanation and prevention steps.
- **2026-03-21** — Completed audit for similarly timed quota updates and reconciled ledgers.

---

## What Happened (Technical)
When the customer increased their quota threshold, the system should:
1) persist the new quota configuration and effective date metadata,
2) emit/consume an update event, and
3) compute proration using the correct rate schedule for the billing period.

During the **2026-03-09 deploy window**, the quota metadata store returned **HTTP 500** for a short interval. The quota-service still emitted the update event, but the metadata write was not committed in time for the invoicing job’s rate/proration computation. The invoicing job **v2.3.1** then:
- retried the invoice job itself, but
- did **not** retry the downstream rate/proration lookup sufficiently, and
- fell back to a default/legacy mapping when quota metadata was missing.

This produced a line item labeled **"overage adjustment"** at the incorrect rate.

---

## Root Cause
**Primary root cause:** Invoicing job **v2.3.1** treated missing quota metadata as a non-fatal condition and used a **default/legacy rate fallback** rather than enforcing consistency (retry/backoff) for proration-required invoices.

**Contributing factors:**
- Intermittent metadata store **500s** during a deploy window.
- A brittle contract between quota-service metadata persistence and the invoicing pipeline’s assumptions about metadata availability.
- Insufficient alerting on "fallback rate mapping used" events (logged but not paged/alerted).

---

## Customer Resolution
- Finance issued a **manual credit** for the incorrect portion of the "overage adjustment" line item (issued **2026-03-19**).
- Customer received:
  - confirmation that the charge was due to an internal proration/rate-lookup error,
  - credit confirmation details, and
  - a summary of prevention steps (hotfix + monitoring).

---

## Fix Implemented
### Engineering changes (deployed)
- **Invoicing job v2.3.2** (deployed **2026-03-18**):
  - Retries rate/proration lookup with exponential backoff when quota metadata is missing.
  - Prevents use of legacy/default fallback mapping for proration-required paths; instead marks invoice generation as "retryable" until metadata is available or a max retry threshold is exceeded.
  - Adds structured logging for "metadata missing" and "fallback prevented" outcomes.

### Monitoring/alerting (added)
- New metric: `invoicing.rate_lookup.fallback_attempted` (should be ~0)
- New alert: page on sustained nonzero fallback attempts over 5 minutes in prod.

---

## Finance & RevRec Guidance (Operational)
When a customer reports a sudden post-quota-change invoice increase with an "overage adjustment" line:
1) Check whether a quota threshold change occurred within 48 hours before invoice generation.
2) Ask Engineering to confirm whether metadata was missing/fallback used.
3) If confirmed, issue a manual credit and tag it as: **Billing correction — proration/rate lookup**.

### Minimum evidence to capture (for audit)
- Invoice ID (e.g., invoice_82419)
- Ledger snapshot (line item details)
- Console quota change timestamp
- Region/environment
- Confirmation of invoicing job version

---

## Appendix A — Key Data Points
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Support ticket | SUP-92477 |
| Customer | Maple Health |
| Tier | Dedicated |
| Region | us-east |
| Console quota change | 2026-03-09 17:42 UTC |
| Invoice generated | 2026-03-10 |
| Line item label | "overage adjustment" |
| Token quantity referenced | ~1.1M tokens |
| Invoicing version observed | v2.3.1 |
| Fix version | v2.3.2 |

---

## Appendix B — Notes on Policy vs. Bug
This incident was a **billing correctness bug**, not a pricing/policy change.
- Policy: proration must reflect the correct rate schedule and effective dates for quota changes.
- Bug: missing metadata led to an incorrect legacy/default rate mapping being applied.

For any future disputes, reference the support ticket and this RCA for justification of credits/refunds when the fallback path is implicated.

- **2026-03-10** — Invoice generated; includes **"overage adjustment"** line item.
- **2026-03-11** — Support engineering flags known proration edge case: rate/proration lookup can fall back when quota metadata is missing.
- **2026-03-12** — Finance places provisional credit hold pending confirmation.
- **2026-03-15** — Root cause confirmed: quota update event emitted, metadata write failed transiently, invoicing job proceeded with fallback.
- **2026-03-18** — Hotfix deployed: invoicing job **v2.3.2** (retry rate lookup; block fallback on proration-required paths).
- **2026-03-19** — Manual credit issued to Maple Health; customer notified with explanation and prevention steps.
- **2026-03-21** — Completed audit for similarly timed quota updates and reconciled ledgers.

---

## What Happened (Technical)
When the customer increased their quota threshold, the system should:
1) persist the new quota configuration and effective date metadata,
2) emit/consume an update event, and
3) compute proration using the correct rate schedule for the billing period.

During the **2026-03-09 deploy window**, the quota metadata store returned **HTTP 500** for a short interval. The quota-service still emitted the update event, but the metadata write was not committed in time for the invoicing job’s rate/proration computation. The invoicing job **v2.3.1** then:
- retried the invoice job itself, but
- did **not** retry the downstream rate/proration lookup sufficiently, and
- fell back to a default/legacy mapping when quota metadata was missing.

This produced a line item labeled **"overage adjustment"** at the incorrect rate.

---

## Root Cause
**Primary root cause:** Invoicing job **v2.3.1** treated missing quota metadata as a non-fatal condition and used a **default/legacy rate fallback** rather than enforcing consistency (retry/backoff) for proration-required invoices.

**Contributing factors:**
- Intermittent metadata store **500s** during a deploy window.
- A brittle contract between quota-service metadata persistence and the invoicing pipeline’s assumptions about metadata availability.
- Insufficient alerting on "fallback rate mapping used" events (logged but not paged/alerted).

---

## Customer Resolution
- Finance issued a **manual credit** for the incorrect portion of the "overage adjustment" line item (issued **2026-03-19**).
- Customer received:
  - confirmation that the charge was due to an internal proration/rate-lookup error,
  - credit confirmation details, and
  - a summary of prevention steps (hotfix + monitoring).

---

## Fix Implemented
### Engineering changes (deployed)
- **Invoicing job v2.3.2** (deployed **2026-03-18**):
  - Retries rate/proration lookup with exponential backoff when quota metadata is missing.
  - Prevents use of legacy/default fallback mapping for proration-required paths; instead marks invoice generation as "retryable" until metadata is available or a max retry threshold is exceeded.
  - Adds structured logging for "metadata missing" and "fallback prevented" outcomes.

### Monitoring/alerting (added)
- New metric: `invoicing.rate_lookup.fallback_attempted` (should be ~0)
- New alert: page on sustained nonzero fallback attempts over 5 minutes in prod.

---

## Finance & RevRec Guidance (Operational)
When a customer reports a sudden post-quota-change invoice increase with an "overage adjustment" line:
1) Check whether a quota threshold change occurred within 48 hours before invoice generation.
2) Ask Engineering to confirm whether metadata was missing/fallback used.
3) If confirmed, issue a manual credit and tag it as: **Billing correction — proration/rate lookup**.

### Minimum evidence to capture (for audit)
- Invoice ID (e.g., invoice_82419)
- Ledger snapshot (line item details)
- Console quota change timestamp
- Region/environment
- Confirmation of invoicing job version

---

## Appendix A — Key Data Points
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Support ticket | SUP-92477 |
| Customer | Maple Health |
| Tier | Dedicated |
| Region | us-east |
| Console quota change | 2026-03-09 17:42 UTC |
| Invoice generated | 2026-03-10 |
| Line item label | "overage adjustment" |
| Token quantity referenced | ~1.1M tokens |
| Invoicing version observed | v2.3.1 |
| Fix version | v2.3.2 |

---

## Appendix B — Notes on Policy vs. Bug
This incident was a **billing correctness bug**, not a pricing/policy change.
- Policy: proration must reflect the correct rate schedule and effective dates for quota changes.
- Bug: missing metadata led to an incorrect legacy/default rate mapping being applied.

For any future disputes, reference the support ticket and this RCA for justification of credits/refunds when the fallback path is implicated.

source.title: Customer Commitment Variance Resolution and Forecasting SOP
source.author: Maya Chen
source.content:
Overview
This Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) outlines the end-to-end process for identifying, triaging, and resolving variance between contracted customer commitments and our revenue/usage forecasts. It also documents how procurement changes, contract amendments, and billing timing anomalies should flow into the financial forecast and revenue recognition pipeline. The goal is to reduce forecast drift to < 2% quarter-on-quarter for enterprise contracted ARR and to ensure consistent handoffs between Sales, Legal, Customer Success, and Finance.

Scope
- Applies to all customer commitments that trigger revenue recognition or forecast adjustments: new enterprise commitments, dedicated capacity reservations, committed usage plans, multi-year subscriptions, and large one-off professional services agreements.
- Excludes ad-hoc usage consumption with no contractual minimums (these are handled through standard usage billing process).

Definitions
- Contracted Commitment: A signed agreement with clear financial terms (ARR, MRR, committed tokens/minutes of capacity).
- Forecast Adjustment Event (FAE): Any trigger that should update the rolling forecast within 48 hours (examples below).
- Revenue Trigger Date: Date when revenue must be recognized per contract and applicable revenue recognition policy.

Common Forecast Adjustment Events (FAEs)
1) Contract Amendment (term change, volume change, price change)
2) Early termination or cancellation notice
3) Migration from hosted to dedicated/private deployment (affects billing cadence and provisioning cost)
4) Large procurement/CapEx request tied to customer onboarding (increases COGS allocation)
5) Billing disputes or credit notes exceeding $10k

Ownership and Roles
- Sales Operations: Validate contract economics, tag deals in CRM with forecast-impact flag, initial communication of FAE.
- Legal: Provide amendment documentation, confirm effective dates, and escalation on ambiguous clauses.
- Customer Success (CS): Confirm customer intent (evidence of migration, usage patterns) and operational handoff.
- Finance (Forecasting Team): Update rolling forecast, model P&L, and push changes to FP&A cube. Owner: Maya Chen. SLA: initial forecast update within 48 hours of receiving completed FAE packet.
- Billing & RevRec: Validate invoicing cadence, prepare revenue recognition entries, and run deferred revenue adjustments. SLA: complete accounting entry within 5 business days after Finance modeling.

FAE Intake Packet (required for all events)
- Signed contract/amendment PDF (required)
- CRM deal ID and change log
- Effective date and billing cadence (monthly/quarterly/annual/one-time)
- Expected MRR/ARR delta and duration (CSV sample with month-by-month expected cash flow for 24 months)
- Procurement/PO reference if customer provided PO (attach PO document)
- CS confirmation email thread evidencing customer intent
- Reviewer checklist completed by Sales Ops (template below)

Reviewer Checklist (short form)
1) Does the amendment change billing cadence? Yes/No
2) Is there a credit, refund, or one-time invoice? Yes/No (amount)
3) Is KMS/HSM or data-residency impacted? Yes/No
4) Does this trigger separate COGS or provisioning CapEx? Yes/No
5) Is there a termination or clawback clause? Yes/No

Triage and Priority Matrix
- Priority 1 (P1): Unexpected cancellations > 5% of ARR or amendments that reduce committed revenue by > $250k within 12 months. Response: immediate finance/leadership review, impact memo within 24 hours.
- Priority 2 (P2): Amendments changing billing cadence or multi-month deferrals > $50k. Response: model update in 48 hours, accounting adjustment within 5 business days.
- Priority 3 (P3): All other contract adjustments under $50k or administrative changes. Response: process within regular weekly forecast cadence.

Forecast Adjustment Procedures (step-by-step)
1) Intake: Sales Ops submits FAE packet to finance-forecasting@redwood.com and tags the deal in CRM with label: FAE_PENDING.
2) Validation (within 24 hours): Finance confirms completeness of packet. If incomplete, send Request for Info (RFI) to Sales Ops with 24-hour turnaround expectation.
3) Modeling (within 48 hours of complete packet): Finance runs two scenarios: (A) Best estimate (most likely) month-by-month cash/ARR delta and (B) Downside (customer does not renew after current term). Attach sensitivity to base case.
4) Governance (as required): For P1/P2 events, present model to Finance Leadership and Legal for sign-off. Sign-off required for revenue impacts > $100k.
5) Forecast Update: Push to rolling 13-month and 24-month FP&A cubes. Annotate forecast line with rationale and link to FAE packet.
6) RevRec Handoff: Notify Billing & RevRec team via revrec-ops@redwood.com to prepare journal entries. Include recommended recognition schedule and any deferral journal templates.
7) Close Loop: CS confirms operational outcome after 30 days (migration complete/cancellation processed). Finance validates realized revenue vs. forecast and records variance reason code.

RevRec and Billing Treatment Guidance
- Billing cadence change from annual -> monthly: defer revenue recognition according to the new billing schedule; the forecast should reflect the expected collection pattern but RevRec should follow ASC 606 principles and our internal revenue policy.
- Migration to Dedicated with upfront hardware pass-through: Recognize pass-through revenue on billing receipt unless contract states otherwise; allocate associated COGS to provisioning cost center 7200.
- Credits and disputes > $10k: Hold in AR-dispute bucket and adjust forecast to remove disputed amounts until dispute resolves.

Example Journal Entry Templates
- Upfront annual prepayment (customer pays $120k on Jan 1 for 12 months):
  Dr Cash 120,000
  Cr Deferred Revenue 120,000
  Monthly recognition post invoice: Dr Deferred Revenue 10,000
                                 Cr Revenue 10,000
- Amendment reduces ARR by $30k effective Apr 1 (quarterly billing):
  Dr Revenue Refund Reserve 30,000
  Cr Accounts Receivable or Cash 30,000 (as applicable)
  Adjust MRR/ARR in FP&A cube with note: "ARR reduced per contract amendment ID XYZ"

Forecast Adjustment Matrix (quick reference)
| Event Type | Model Action | Accounting Action | Ownership | SLA |
| Contract amendment (term/price) | Update ARR curve, provide month-level CSV | Prepare deferral/accelerated entries if needed | Sales Ops / Finance | 48h to model, 5d to post |
| Cancellation > 30 days notice | Zero future renewal probability; adjust churn | Reverse deferred revenue for unused portion | CS / Finance | 24h flag, 5d posting |
| Billing dispute > $10k | Remove disputed amount from near-term cash forecast | Move to AR-dispute and hold recognition | Billing / Finance | 48h acknowledge |
| Migration hosted -> dedicated | Shift revenue recognition to new contract terms; adjust COGS | Create provisioning COGS forecast and journal | CS / Procurement / Finance | 48h model update |

Tools and Templates
- Required templates live in /confluence/finance-and-legal/templates: FAE_packet_template.xlsx, amendment_review_checklist.xlsx, forecast_upload_template.csv
- Systems: CRM (Salesforce) must be updated with custom field FAE_FLAG; FP&A cube is hosted in Anaplan (model: FP&A_Rolling_Forecast_V2); RevRec journals recorded in NetSuite (subledger: REVREC).

Escalation and Dispute Resolution Path
1) If Sales Ops and Finance disagree on recognition timing, open an Escalation Ticket in JIRA: FIN-ESCALATION and assign to Finance Lead (Maya Chen).
2) If Legal identifies ambiguous contract language, route to Legal Counsel (Priya Nair) and mark the FAE as P2 until resolved.
3) For disputes impacting public financial statements or > $1M, notify the CFO and Controller within 24 hours and prepare a board-facing memo.

Monitoring and Post-Mortem
- Monthly: Finance runs a variance report comparing realized revenue to forecast with top 10 drivers and posts the report in /confluence/finance-and-legal/budgeting-and-forecasting/monthly-variance-report.
- Quarterly: Conduct a Forecast Accuracy Review (owner: FP&A) that reviews all P1 events, lessons learned, and opportunities to change playbook thresholds.

Change History and Versioning
- 2026-01-12: Document created by Maya Chen.
- 2026-02-05: Added priority matrix and intake packet checklist (reviewers: Diego Alvarez, Priya Nair).
- 2026-03-02: Clarified RevRec treatment for dedicated migrations and added journal templates.

Appendices
A. Sample CSV format for FAE month-by-month cash flow (header only):
  month,expected_cash,expected_revenue,contract_id,notes
B. Contact directory:
  - Finance Forecasting (owner): maya.chen@redwood.com
  - RevRec Ops: revrec-ops@redwood.com
  - Sales Ops: sales-ops@redwood.com
  - Legal Counsel: priya.nair@redwood.com

Questions or suggested updates should be emailed to finance-ops@redwood.com.

source.title: Capacity Contracting and Financial Governance Guidance
source.author: Maya Chen
source.body:
Purpose:

Provide concrete governance for how Redwood ties multi-month capacity contracts to budgeting, procurement, legal review, and revenue recognition. This guidance is intended for Finance, Legal, Procurement, Sales, and Platform teams responsible for capacity commitments.

Executive summary:
- Treat all multi-month reserved GPU capacity agreements as cross-functional commitments that require synchronized forecasting, procurement timing, and revrec mapping.
- Establish predictable thresholds and a clear approval chain to protect financial runway while enabling sales to secure competitive terms for customers.
- Define operational steps to recover value from unused capacity and to limit exposure from long-term discounts.

Definitions and thresholds:
- Normalized Capacity Unit (CU): defined as a normalized GPU serving slot used for financial planning.
- Strategic Commitment: any deal or purchase that meets one or more: total contract value >= $300,000; term >= 12 months; or new private deployment requiring managed control plane.
- Short Commitment: term < 12 months and value < $300,000.

Forecasting requirements:
1) Data inputs and owners:
   - Sales: deal pipeline and expected go-live dates (weekly updates).
   - Product: expected feature-driven demand multipliers for each customer cohort (quarterly).
   - Eng-SRE: measured CU utilization by cluster and projected p95 latency impact (daily metrics, monthly summary).
   - Finance: converts utilization into cashflow impacts and runway scenarios (monthly).
2) Minimum forecast horizon: 12 months rolling, with a 24-month strategic view for any Strategic Commitment.

Procurement and purchase timing:
- Preferred procurement window: align purchases to next fiscal quarter planning cycle unless emergency scale required by SRE.
- For Strategic Commitments, procurements should start RFP and pricing negotiation at least 60 days before the expected capacity start date.
- Payment options must be assessed for their RevRec impact: prepaid discounts versus monthly billing. Finance will simulate cashflow and deferred revenue impacts before approval.

Approval matrix (roles and thresholds):
- Procurement lead: approves Short Commitments up to $300k total value.
- Finance Director: approves Strategic Commitments below $1M and certifies runway impact.
- CFO: approves Strategic Commitments >= $1M or any deal that reduces runway coverage below the target threshold defined by Finance.
- Legal: must sign off on termination, exit, data residency, and SLA credit language for all Strategic Commitments.

Contract language standards (minimum required clauses):
- Service start definition: explicit acceptance tests or go-live criteria that trigger the start of service and billing.
- SLA credit mechanics: clear measurement window, credit calculation, and dispute resolution timing.
- Reuse and allocation: where permissible, include a clause allowing Redwood to reallocate or resell unused committed capacity after a notice period, subject to customer privacy constraints.
- Buyout option: standardized buyout formula for early termination to avoid open-ended exposure.

Revenue recognition guidance:
- Prepaid capacity fees: record as deferred revenue and recognize ratably over the contractual service period beginning at the service start.
- Monthly invoiced capacity: recognize as revenue in the period services are rendered.
- Implementation fees for private deployments: recognize on delivery milestones if separable; otherwise, amortize over the contractual period.
- Discounts tied to term length: clearly map discount percentage to gross contract value, and reflect the net price in revrec modeling.

Unused capacity and breakage mitigation:
- 0	60 days after go-live: account teams and SRE collaborate to migrate eligible workloads into the committed pool.
- 61	120 days: Finance and Sales may offer transferable credits for other internal customers or partner marketplace placement where contract permits.
- >120 days: leadership decision to apply a contractual buyout, marketing resale, or write-down. All outcomes must be recorded in the Contract Exceptions log.

Discount policy and controls:
- Standard term discounts (illustrative): 12 months = 8% max; 24 months = 16% max; 36+ months = 20% max.
- Any nonstandard commercial terms or increased discounts require a Contract Exceptions entry and sign-off from Finance Director and Legal Counsel.

Operational runbooks (step-by-step):
- Monthly alignment runbook (owner: Finance)
  1. Pull pipeline from CRM and map to expected CU start dates.
  2. Reconcile with SRE utilization dashboard and compute additional CU needs.
  3. Publish Capacity Funding Memo: required funding, recommended payment terms, and runway impact.
  4. If procurement is recommended, open Jira procurement ticket and attach Capacity Funding Memo.
- Contract close runbook (owner: Legal + Finance)
  1. Validate contract metadata: start date, billing cadence, termination language, and SLA definitions.
  2. Confirm RevRec treatment and chart of accounts mapping.
  3. Enter the contract into the RevRec system with monthly recognition schedule.

Reporting and KPIs:
- Monthly Capacity Financial Report must include: total committed CUs, utilization rate, deferred revenue for capacity, runway coverage months, and top 10 at-risk contracts.
- KPIs: coverage ratio (target >= 6 months), average utilization by cluster (goal > 70%), breakage rate (goal < 5% of committed spend).

Escalation and exceptions:
- If coverage ratio drops below 4 months, Finance and Procurement execute an emergency review and produce mitigation options within 7 business days.
- All Contract Exceptions are reviewed quarterly by a cross-functional panel (Finance, Legal, Sales, Eng-SRE).

Implementation actions (next 90 days):
- Update the standard contract template to add reuse and buyout language (owner: Legal).
- Automate the CU conversion step inside the Forecast workbook using per-route telemetry APIs (owner: Finance + Eng Platform).
- Pilot a transfer-credit program for internal teams to absorb unused capacity (owner: Revenue Ops).

Revision history:
- 2026-01-10: Document created by Maya Chen.
- 2026-03-01: Clarified procurement windows and added buyout guidance.

Primary contacts and owners:
- Finance owner: Maya Chen (mchen@redwood.example)
- Procurement contact: Evan Park (epark@redwood.example)
- Legal contact: Priya Natarajan (pnatarajan@redwood.example)

End of guidance.
