source.title: Metering → Invoice Reconciliation Playbook (Hosted API + Dedicated)
source.author: Emily Stone
source.content:
## Purpose
This playbook defines how Redwood Inference reconciles customer invoices back to underlying metering data.

It is designed to:
- Detect billing-impacting metering issues early (daily/weekly) and before invoices are sent (month-end).
- Provide a repeatable, audit-ready trail from: **request logs → usage events → billing ledger → invoice lines → customer usage exports**.
- Standardize discrepancy classification and remediation (forward fixes, credits, backfills) with clear sign-offs.

**Applies to:**
- Hosted API (tokens/requests, embeddings, reranking)
- Dedicated (GPU-seconds)

**Not covered:** tax, FX, payment collection, revenue allocation. For month-end close controls, see *Month-End Close: Usage Reconciliation Checklist*.

---

## Definitions (canonical)
**Billing Ledger:** The internal finance-grade table used to generate invoice line items. This is the **source of truth for invoicing** once the billing freeze occurs.

**Usage Events:** Telemetry events emitted per request (or per logical request segment) that contain metered quantities (e.g., billed_tokens, requests, cache_hit attribution, gpu_seconds).

**Customer Usage Export / Usage API:** Customer-facing aggregation derived from usage events and/or ledger with documented lag and schema. Used for customer validation and dispute resolution.

**Reconciliation Grain:**
- Hosted API: account_id × usage_date (UTC) × region × model × product_route (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank) × price_plan
- Dedicated: account_id × usage_date (UTC) × pool_id × region × sku

**Freeze Window:** A time after which we stop changing ledger totals for the close period except via an approved adjustment process. (See Finance Slack thread and the month-end checklist.)

---

## Source-of-truth hierarchy
Use this hierarchy to decide which number “wins” in an investigation:
1) **Invoice PDF / issued invoice** (immutable customer artifact)
2) **Billing Ledger for the invoiced period** (invoice derivation) 
3) **Ledger reconciliation export** (Finance reconciler-friendly view of #2)
4) **Usage Events (raw + deduped)** (root cause and forward-fix validation)
5) **Customer Usage API/Exports** (customer observability; may lag)

If customer exports differ from ledger, treat it as either (a) a known lag/definition difference, or (b) a product bug requiring customer communication and a fix.

---

## Roles, required sign-offs, and escalation
### Primary owners
- **Finance/RevRec Owner:** Emily Stone
- **FP&A / Margin Owner:** Rishi Malhotra (only when margin model assumptions change)
- **Telemetry Engineering:** Logan Wright (EM), Nadia Rahman (spec/attribution)
- **Billing Hooks / Quotas:** Cole Summers
- **Legal (usage terms / disputes):** Michael Grant (GC), Sofia Mendes (Commercial Counsel)

### Sign-off rules (minimum)
| Decision / Output | Required approvers |
|---|---|
| Close period reconciliation complete | Emily Stone + (delegate from Telemetry Eng) |
| Any change to metering definitions affecting billed quantities | Emily Stone + Logan Wright + Nadia Rahman + Michael Grant (if customer-facing definitions change) |
| Any retroactive change to ledger totals for a closed/issued invoice | Laura Bennett + Emily Stone + Michael Grant |
| Customer credit/refund due to metering error (>$10k or >2% of invoice) | Laura Bennett + Michael Grant |
| Backfill of usage events that could change customer exports for prior periods | Emily Stone + Logan Wright + Product owner (Nikhil Sharma for Hosted API; Ethan Park for Dedicated) |

### Escalation triggers
Escalate to Finance + Engineering same business day if any are true:
- **Ledger vs Usage Events drift** exceeds thresholds (see “Thresholds” below)
- Duplicate rate, null dimension rate, or freshness SLO breach persists >2 hours
- Any top-20 revenue account shows unexplained variance >$500/day or >1% of daily spend

---

## Access & tooling prerequisites
- Access to restricted datasets per *Metering Data Retention and Access Controls*.
- Ability to run reconciliation queries in the approved warehouse (Snowflake) or via the Finance reconciliation export.
- Links:
  - Meter sanity dashboard (Console/Grafana)
  - Ledger reconciliation export table/view
  - Usage API export endpoint (for customer-visible numbers)

**Audit requirement:** All reconciliation exports shared externally must come from the approved export view and be attached to the ticket/case with timestamp.

---

## Cadence
### Daily (operational)
Goal: catch pipeline regressions before they reach customer invoices.
- Run automated sanity checks (freshness, duplicates, null dimensions, drift).
- Triage any alert within 30 minutes during business hours.

### Weekly (control)
Goal: verify stability and trend anomalies.
- Reconcile previous 7 days ledger vs usage events.
- Sample 5–10 accounts across tiers and 2–3 regions.

### Month-end (close)
Goal: produce close-ready evidence package.
- Follow the Month-End Close checklist.
- Confirm freeze window and late-event policy.

---

## Thresholds and SLOs (default)
These are the default control thresholds. Adjustments require Finance + Telemetry Eng approval and must be documented.

### Data freshness
- Usage events availability lag: **p95 ≤ 15 min**, **p99 ≤ 60 min**
- Ledger export availability lag: **≤ 6 hours** (typical), **hard fail if > 12 hours**
- Customer usage export: **≤ 24 hours** (documented customer expectation)

### Correctness/drift
All drift thresholds are computed at the reconciliation grain.
- **Daily drift (Hosted API tokens):** absolute drift ≤ **0.25%** AND ≤ **$250** per account-day
- **Weekly drift:** absolute drift ≤ **0.10%** AND ≤ **$500** per account-week
- **Dedicated GPU-seconds:** absolute drift ≤ **0.20%** AND ≤ **$500** per account-day

### Dimension completeness
- Null rate for required dimensions (account_id, usage_date, product_route, region, model/sku): **< 0.05%**
- Duplicate usage_event_id rate post-dedupe: **< 0.02%**

---

## Step-by-step reconciliation procedure
### Step 0 — Start a reconciliation ticket and scope
Create a ticket (Finance Ops or shared tracker) containing:
- Period (UTC): start_date, end_date
- Product surface: Hosted API and/or Dedicated
- Data sources used (table/view names + snapshot timestamp)
- Whether any meter changes occurred in period (shadow billing, backfills, rollouts)

**Required attachment:** exported drift summary (CSV) from the approved reconciliation view.

---

### Step 1 — Verify freshness & completeness gates
Before comparing numbers, confirm we are not comparing incomplete data.

Checklist:
- Usage events ingestion is current (meets freshness SLO)
- Ledger export is current
- Any known incident windows are annotated (e.g., pipeline delays, regional outages)

If freshness is failing:
1) Pause reconciliation for the impacted window.
2) Page/notify the on-call owner per the meter sanity dashboard spec.
3) Re-run Step 1 after pipeline catch-up.

---

### Step 2 — Confirm dedupe/idempotency posture
Many “overbilling” reports are actually duplicate-event issues during retries/backfills.

Controls:
- Use **deduped usage events** view for aggregates (never raw events) unless investigating.
- Verify dedupe key includes request_id + attempt_id + idempotency_key (as applicable).

Common symptoms:
- Drift spikes aligned to deploys
- Drift concentrated in streaming endpoints
- Duplicate rate increases in one region/cluster

---

### Step 3 — Aggregate usage events to the reconciliation grain
Aggregate usage events to the same grain as the ledger/invoice lines.

**Hosted API:**
- Sum billed_prompt_tokens, billed_completion_tokens, billed_tokens_total
- Count billable_requests
- Track cache_hit tokens and non-cache tokens separately (for attribution/experiments)

**Dedicated:**
- Sum gpu_seconds_billable
- Separate reserved vs burst usage if applicable

Example (pseudo-SQL):
```
SELECT
  account_id,
  DATE_TRUNC('day', usage_ts_utc) AS usage_date,
  region,
  model,
  product_route,
  price_plan,
  SUM(billed_tokens_total) AS evt_billed_tokens,
  SUM(billable_requests) AS evt_requests,
  SUM(cache_hit_tokens) AS evt_cache_hit_tokens,
  COUNT(*) AS evt_rows
FROM usage_events_deduped
WHERE usage_ts_utc >= :start_ts AND usage_ts_utc < :end_ts
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5,6;
```

**Important:** ensure timezone alignment. All reconciliation is done in **UTC** unless explicitly performing a customer-specific export in their billing timezone.

---

### Step 4 — Pull ledger totals for the same grain
Use the approved ledger reconciliation export (preferred) or ledger table (restricted).

Confirm:
- Ledger includes the same dimension keys (or a documented mapping)
- Price plan changes mid-period are represented consistently

Example (pseudo-SQL):
```
SELECT
  account_id,
  usage_date,
  region,
  model,
  product_route,
  price_plan,
  SUM(billed_tokens_total) AS led_billed_tokens,
  SUM(billable_requests) AS led_requests,
  SUM(amount_usd) AS led_amount_usd
FROM billing_ledger_recon_export
WHERE usage_date BETWEEN :start_date AND :end_date
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5,6;
```

---

### Step 5 — Compute drift and classify discrepancies
Join usage-event aggregates to ledger aggregates and compute:
- Token drift: (evt_billed_tokens - led_billed_tokens)
- Request drift
- Amount drift (if ledger contains pricing)

Classification (pick one primary label):
1) **Late-arriving usage events** (events present after ledger cut; freshness breach)
2) **Duplicate events / idempotency regression** (evt > ledger, duplicates elevated)
3) **Retry/fallback double-count** (streaming + server retry; known failure mode)
4) **Missing dimensions** (region/model/route nulls causing mis-bucketing)
5) **Token definition mismatch** (raw vs billed tokens; tool calls/structured output)
6) **Embeddings batch accounting bug** (per-input vs per-request counting)
7) **Timezone boundary mismatch** (customer export vs ledger day boundaries)
8) **Expected business rule difference** (e.g., free tier, minimums, rounding rules)

Decision rule:
- If drift is **within thresholds** and no systematic bias is observed: record as pass.
- If drift is **outside thresholds**: proceed to Step 6.

---

### Step 6 — Cross-check against customer usage API/export
For customer disputes and customer-visible consistency, compare ledger totals to the customer usage export/API.

Procedure:
1) Pull usage export for same account and UTC period.
2) Confirm export schema version and whether it shows **billed_tokens** vs **raw_tokens**.
3) Reconcile at daily grain, then drill down by model/region/route.

If customer usage export differs from ledger:
- Determine whether it is due to **lag** (export behind ledger) or **definition mismatch**.
- If definition mismatch: document which field is correct for customer messaging and ensure docs/SDK are aligned.

---

### Step 7 — Drill-down playbook by failure mode
Use this section when Step 5 flags drift outside thresholds.

#### A) Late-arriving events
Signals:
- Freshness SLO breach
- Drift concentrated in the most recent 24–48 hours

Actions:
- Re-run reconciliation after pipeline catch-up.
- If ledger has already frozen for the period: do **not** change ledger without CFO/RevRec approval.
- Add incident annotation and attach evidence (lag chart + impacted partitions).

#### B) Duplicate events / idempotency regression
Signals:
- Duplicate rate increases
- Drift correlates with partial failures/backfills

Actions:
- Identify duplicate key collisions and affected endpoints.
- Engineering to confirm dedupe window and idempotency key behavior.
- Prefer forward fix + backfill usage events view; do not retroactively re-issue invoices without approval.

#### C) Retry/fallback double-count (Hosted API)
Signals:
- Drift spikes on streaming/chat
- Higher variance in routes using fallback routing

Actions:
- Validate whether both attempts emitted billable events.
- Confirm request_id/attempt_id relationship in logs.
- If confirmed: implement fix + run shadow billing compare before enabling.

#### D) Missing/incorrect dimensions (region/model/route)
Signals:
- Null dimension rate > threshold
- Drift appears as “missing” in one bucket and “extra” in another

Actions:
- Determine whether ledger applies defaults/mappings that usage events do not.
- Backfill dimension fields if needed (with Finance approval if it changes customer exports).

#### E) Token definition mismatch (raw vs billed)
Signals:
- Customer compares invoice to raw token counts from SDK logs
- Tool calls / structured output present

Actions:
- Confirm invoice uses **billed_tokens** per meter spec.
- Ensure customer usage export includes billed_tokens and is documented.
- If mismatch is due to OpenAI-compatible endpoint semantics: coordinate with Product + Docs.

#### F) Embeddings batch accounting
Signals:
- Drift only on embeddings route
- Customers using batched input arrays

Actions:
- Validate whether per-input tokenization is applied consistently.
- Confirm “billable unit” definition in meter spec.
- Run unit tests / regression scenario where batch sizes vary.

#### G) Timezone boundary mismatch (exports)
Signals:
- Customer sees day-over-day spikes at midnight local time

Actions:
- Confirm invoice period is UTC.
- If customer requires a local-time export, provide it as an informational report but do not change invoice basis unless contract specifies.

---

### Step 8 — Decide remediation path (forward fix vs adjustment)
Use this decision tree once root cause is identified.

**Default policy:** prefer **forward fixes** with clear documentation and audit trail. Avoid retroactive invoice changes.

Remediation options:
1) **No action** (variance within threshold; document)
2) **Forward fix only** (metering bug fixed; applies to future usage)
3) **Customer credit** (invoice remains; credit memo or usage credit issued)
4) **Invoice adjustment / re-issue** (rare; requires CFO + Legal)
5) **Backfill** (usage events/customer export corrected; ledger may or may not change depending on freeze)

Minimum documentation for any remediation:
- Root cause summary
- Impacted period + accounts
- Quantified $ impact (best estimate + bounds)
- Customer comms plan (Support/Sales owner)
- Approvals captured (names + date)

---

### Step 9 — Produce an audit-ready evidence package
Attach to the reconciliation ticket:
- Drift report (by account/day/model/region/route)
- Freshness and completeness charts for the period
- Any incident annotations
- Query fingerprints (table/view names + snapshot timestamp)
- Final sign-off comment (RevRec + Eng)

Retention:
- Store evidence per retention policy; do not export raw request logs.

---

## Appendix A — Minimal reconciliation checklist (quick pass)
Use for weekly spot-checks.
1) Confirm freshness SLO pass
2) Run ledger vs usage events drift (last 7 days)
3) Sample 10 accounts (2 enterprise, 5 mid-market, 3 self-serve)
4) Validate top 5 models per account
5) Confirm null rate and duplicate rate are under thresholds
6) Record pass/fail and open investigations

---

## Appendix B — Customer dispute workflow integration
When Support/Sales opens a billing dispute:
1) Require: invoice id, account id, disputed period, customer’s comparison method (SDK logs vs export)
2) Run Step 6 first (customer usage export alignment)
3) If discrepancy persists, proceed Steps 3–5 at a narrower scope
4) Provide customer-facing explanation using approved language (Legal playbook)
5) If credit is recommended, route for approval per sign-off rules

---

## Appendix C — Known recurring failure modes (historical)
- Streaming retry/fallback double-counting when both attempts emitted billable events
- Missing region dimension leading to mis-priced regional rates and margin attribution errors
- Embeddings batched requests counted per-request instead of per-input tokenization
- Customer export timezone misunderstanding (UTC invoice vs local-time dashboards)

---

## Change control
Any change to metering definitions or ledger derivation logic must follow:
- Shadow billing compare for at least one full business cycle (typically 7 days)
- Documented before/after diffs and expected variance
- Finance/RevRec sign-off prior to enabling invoice-impacting behavior

(See the metering change control process ticket and the meter sanity dashboard spec for alerting requirements.)

title: Metering → Invoice Reconciliation Playbook (Hosted API + Dedicated)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook defines how Redwood Inference reconciles customer invoices back to underlying metering data.

It is designed to:
- Detect billing-impacting metering issues early (daily/weekly) and before invoices are sent (month-end).
- Provide a repeatable, audit-ready trail from: **request logs → usage events → billing ledger → invoice lines → customer usage exports**.
- Standardize discrepancy classification and remediation (forward fixes, credits, backfills) with clear sign-offs.

**Applies to:**
- Hosted API (tokens/requests, embeddings, reranking)
- Dedicated (GPU-seconds)

**Not covered:** tax, FX, payment collection, revenue allocation. For month-end close controls, see *Month-End Close: Usage Reconciliation Checklist*.

---

## Definitions (canonical)
**Billing Ledger:** The internal finance-grade table used to generate invoice line items. This is the **source of truth for invoicing** once the billing freeze occurs.

**Usage Events:** Telemetry events emitted per request (or per logical request segment) that contain metered quantities (e.g., billed_tokens, requests, cache_hit attribution, gpu_seconds).

**Customer Usage Export / Usage API:** Customer-facing aggregation derived from usage events and/or ledger with documented lag and schema. Used for customer validation and dispute resolution.

**Reconciliation Grain:**
- Hosted API: account_id × usage_date (UTC) × region × model × product_route (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank) × price_plan
- Dedicated: account_id × usage_date (UTC) × pool_id × region × sku

**Freeze Window:** A time after which we stop changing ledger totals for the close period except via an approved adjustment process. (See Finance Slack thread and the month-end checklist.)

---

## Source-of-truth hierarchy
Use this hierarchy to decide which number “wins” in an investigation:
1) **Invoice PDF / issued invoice** (immutable customer artifact)
2) **Billing Ledger for the invoiced period** (invoice derivation) 
3) **Ledger reconciliation export** (Finance reconciler-friendly view of #2)
4) **Usage Events (raw + deduped)** (root cause and forward-fix validation)
5) **Customer Usage API/Exports** (customer observability; may lag)

If customer exports differ from ledger, treat it as either (a) a known lag/definition difference, or (b) a product bug requiring customer communication and a fix.

---

## Roles, required sign-offs, and escalation
### Primary owners
- **Finance/RevRec Owner:** Emily Stone
- **FP&A / Margin Owner:** Rishi Malhotra (only when margin model assumptions change)
- **Telemetry Engineering:** Logan Wright (EM), Nadia Rahman (spec/attribution)
- **Billing Hooks / Quotas:** Cole Summers
- **Legal (usage terms / disputes):** Michael Grant (GC), Sofia Mendes (Commercial Counsel)

### Sign-off rules (minimum)
| Decision / Output | Required approvers |
|---|---|
| Close period reconciliation complete | Emily Stone + (delegate from Telemetry Eng) |
| Any change to metering definitions affecting billed quantities | Emily Stone + Logan Wright + Nadia Rahman + Michael Grant (if customer-facing definitions change) |
| Any retroactive change to ledger totals for a closed/issued invoice | Laura Bennett + Emily Stone + Michael Grant |
| Customer credit/refund due to metering error (>$10k or >2% of invoice) | Laura Bennett + Michael Grant |
| Backfill of usage events that could change customer exports for prior periods | Emily Stone + Logan Wright + Product owner (Nikhil Sharma for Hosted API; Ethan Park for Dedicated) |

### Escalation triggers
Escalate to Finance + Engineering same business day if any are true:
- **Ledger vs Usage Events drift** exceeds thresholds (see “Thresholds” below)
- Duplicate rate, null dimension rate, or freshness SLO breach persists >2 hours
- Any top-20 revenue account shows unexplained variance >$500/day or >1% of daily spend

---

## Access & tooling prerequisites
- Access to restricted datasets per *Metering Data Retention and Access Controls*.
- Ability to run reconciliation queries in the approved warehouse (Snowflake) or via the Finance reconciliation export.
- Links:
  - Meter sanity dashboard (Console/Grafana)
  - Ledger reconciliation export table/view
  - Usage API export endpoint (for customer-visible numbers)

**Audit requirement:** All reconciliation exports shared externally must come from the approved export view and be attached to the ticket/case with timestamp.

---

## Cadence
### Daily (operational)
Goal: catch pipeline regressions before they reach customer invoices.
- Run automated sanity checks (freshness, duplicates, null dimensions, drift).
- Triage any alert within 30 minutes during business hours.

### Weekly (control)
Goal: verify stability and trend anomalies.
- Reconcile previous 7 days ledger vs usage events.
- Sample 5–10 accounts across tiers and 2–3 regions.

### Month-end (close)
Goal: produce close-ready evidence package.
- Follow the Month-End Close checklist.
- Confirm freeze window and late-event policy.

---

## Thresholds and SLOs (default)
These are the default control thresholds. Adjustments require Finance + Telemetry Eng approval and must be documented.

### Data freshness
- Usage events availability lag: **p95 ≤ 15 min**, **p99 ≤ 60 min**
- Ledger export availability lag: **≤ 6 hours** (typical), **hard fail if > 12 hours**
- Customer usage export: **≤ 24 hours** (documented customer expectation)

### Correctness/drift
All drift thresholds are computed at the reconciliation grain.
- **Daily drift (Hosted API tokens):** absolute drift ≤ **0.25%** AND ≤ **$250** per account-day
- **Weekly drift:** absolute drift ≤ **0.10%** AND ≤ **$500** per account-week
- **Dedicated GPU-seconds:** absolute drift ≤ **0.20%** AND ≤ **$500** per account-day

### Dimension completeness
- Null rate for required dimensions (account_id, usage_date, product_route, region, model/sku): **< 0.05%**
- Duplicate usage_event_id rate post-dedupe: **< 0.02%**

---

## Step-by-step reconciliation procedure
### Step 0 — Start a reconciliation ticket and scope
Create a ticket (Finance Ops or shared tracker) containing:
- Period (UTC): start_date, end_date
- Product surface: Hosted API and/or Dedicated
- Data sources used (table/view names + snapshot timestamp)
- Whether any meter changes occurred in period (shadow billing, backfills, rollouts)

**Required attachment:** exported drift summary (CSV) from the approved reconciliation view.

---

### Step 1 — Verify freshness & completeness gates
Before comparing numbers, confirm we are not comparing incomplete data.

Checklist:
- Usage events ingestion is current (meets freshness SLO)
- Ledger export is current
- Any known incident windows are annotated (e.g., pipeline delays, regional outages)

If freshness is failing:
1) Pause reconciliation for the impacted window.
2) Page/notify the on-call owner per the meter sanity dashboard spec.
3) Re-run Step 1 after pipeline catch-up.

---

### Step 2 — Confirm dedupe/idempotency posture
Many “overbilling” reports are actually duplicate-event issues during retries/backfills.

Controls:
- Use **deduped usage events** view for aggregates (never raw events) unless investigating.
- Verify dedupe key includes request_id + attempt_id + idempotency_key (as applicable).

Common symptoms:
- Drift spikes aligned to deploys
- Drift concentrated in streaming endpoints
- Duplicate rate increases in one region/cluster

---

### Step 3 — Aggregate usage events to the reconciliation grain
Aggregate usage events to the same grain as the ledger/invoice lines.

**Hosted API:**
- Sum billed_prompt_tokens, billed_completion_tokens, billed_tokens_total
- Count billable_requests
- Track cache_hit tokens and non-cache tokens separately (for attribution/experiments)

**Dedicated:**
- Sum gpu_seconds_billable
- Separate reserved vs burst usage if applicable

Example (pseudo-SQL):
```
SELECT
  account_id,
  DATE_TRUNC('day', usage_ts_utc) AS usage_date,
  region,
  model,
  product_route,
  price_plan,
  SUM(billed_tokens_total) AS evt_billed_tokens,
  SUM(billable_requests) AS evt_requests,
  SUM(cache_hit_tokens) AS evt_cache_hit_tokens,
  COUNT(*) AS evt_rows
FROM usage_events_deduped
WHERE usage_ts_utc >= :start_ts AND usage_ts_utc < :end_ts
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5,6;
```

**Important:** ensure timezone alignment. All reconciliation is done in **UTC** unless explicitly performing a customer-specific export in their billing timezone.

---

### Step 4 — Pull ledger totals for the same grain
Use the approved ledger reconciliation export (preferred) or ledger table (restricted).

Confirm:
- Ledger includes the same dimension keys (or a documented mapping)
- Price plan changes mid-period are represented consistently

Example (pseudo-SQL):
```
SELECT
  account_id,
  usage_date,
  region,
  model,
  product_route,
  price_plan,
  SUM(billed_tokens_total) AS led_billed_tokens,
  SUM(billable_requests) AS led_requests,
  SUM(amount_usd) AS led_amount_usd
FROM billing_ledger_recon_export
WHERE usage_date BETWEEN :start_date AND :end_date
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5,6;
```

---

### Step 5 — Compute drift and classify discrepancies
Join usage-event aggregates to ledger aggregates and compute:
- Token drift: (evt_billed_tokens - led_billed_tokens)
- Request drift
- Amount drift (if ledger contains pricing)

Classification (pick one primary label):
1) **Late-arriving usage events** (events present after ledger cut; freshness breach)
2) **Duplicate events / idempotency regression** (evt > ledger, duplicates elevated)
3) **Retry/fallback double-count** (streaming + server retry; known failure mode)
4) **Missing dimensions** (region/model/route nulls causing mis-bucketing)
5) **Token definition mismatch** (raw vs billed tokens; tool calls/structured output)
6) **Embeddings batch accounting bug** (per-input vs per-request counting)
7) **Timezone boundary mismatch** (customer export vs ledger day boundaries)
8) **Expected business rule difference** (e.g., free tier, minimums, rounding rules)

Decision rule:
- If drift is **within thresholds** and no systematic bias is observed: record as pass.
- If drift is **outside thresholds**: proceed to Step 6.

---

### Step 6 — Cross-check against customer usage API/export
For customer disputes and customer-visible consistency, compare ledger totals to the customer usage export/API.

Procedure:
1) Pull usage export for same account and UTC period.
2) Confirm export schema version and whether it shows **billed_tokens** vs **raw_tokens**.
3) Reconcile at daily grain, then drill down by model/region/route.

If customer usage export differs from ledger:
- Determine whether it is due to **lag** (export behind ledger) or **definition mismatch**.
- If definition mismatch: document which field is correct for customer messaging and ensure docs/SDK are aligned.

---

### Step 7 — Drill-down playbook by failure mode
Use this section when Step 5 flags drift outside thresholds.

#### A) Late-arriving events
Signals:
- Freshness SLO breach
- Drift concentrated in the most recent 24–48 hours

Actions:
- Re-run reconciliation after pipeline catch-up.
- If ledger has already frozen for the period: do **not** change ledger without CFO/RevRec approval.
- Add incident annotation and attach evidence (lag chart + impacted partitions).

#### B) Duplicate events / idempotency regression
Signals:
- Duplicate rate increases
- Drift correlates with partial failures/backfills

Actions:
- Identify duplicate key collisions and affected endpoints.
- Engineering to confirm dedupe window and idempotency key behavior.
- Prefer forward fix + backfill usage events view; do not retroactively re-issue invoices without approval.

#### C) Retry/fallback double-count (Hosted API)
Signals:
- Drift spikes on streaming/chat
- Higher variance in routes using fallback routing

Actions:
- Validate whether both attempts emitted billable events.
- Confirm request_id/attempt_id relationship in logs.
- If confirmed: implement fix + run shadow billing compare before enabling.

#### D) Missing/incorrect dimensions (region/model/route)
Signals:
- Null dimension rate > threshold
- Drift appears as “missing” in one bucket and “extra” in another

Actions:
- Determine whether ledger applies defaults/mappings that usage events do not.
- Backfill dimension fields if needed (with Finance approval if it changes customer exports).

#### E) Token definition mismatch (raw vs billed)
Signals:
- Customer compares invoice to raw token counts from SDK logs
- Tool calls / structured output present

Actions:
- Confirm invoice uses **billed_tokens** per meter spec.
- Ensure customer usage export includes billed_tokens and is documented.
- If mismatch is due to OpenAI-compatible endpoint semantics: coordinate with Product + Docs.

#### F) Embeddings batch accounting
Signals:
- Drift only on embeddings route
- Customers using batched input arrays

Actions:
- Validate whether per-input tokenization is applied consistently.
- Confirm “billable unit” definition in meter spec.
- Run unit tests / regression scenario where batch sizes vary.

#### G) Timezone boundary mismatch (exports)
Signals:
- Customer sees day-over-day spikes at midnight local time

Actions:
- Confirm invoice period is UTC.
- If customer requires a local-time export, provide it as an informational report but do not change invoice basis unless contract specifies.

---

### Step 8 — Decide remediation path (forward fix vs adjustment)
Use this decision tree once root cause is identified.

**Default policy:** prefer **forward fixes** with clear documentation and audit trail. Avoid retroactive invoice changes.

Remediation options:
1) **No action** (variance within threshold; document)
2) **Forward fix only** (metering bug fixed; applies to future usage)
3) **Customer credit** (invoice remains; credit memo or usage credit issued)
4) **Invoice adjustment / re-issue** (rare; requires CFO + Legal)
5) **Backfill** (usage events/customer export corrected; ledger may or may not change depending on freeze)

Minimum documentation for any remediation:
- Root cause summary
- Impacted period + accounts
- Quantified $ impact (best estimate + bounds)
- Customer comms plan (Support/Sales owner)
- Approvals captured (names + date)

---

### Step 9 — Produce an audit-ready evidence package
Attach to the reconciliation ticket:
- Drift report (by account/day/model/region/route)
- Freshness and completeness charts for the period
- Any incident annotations
- Query fingerprints (table/view names + snapshot timestamp)
- Final sign-off comment (RevRec + Eng)

Retention:
- Store evidence per retention policy; do not export raw request logs.

---

## Appendix A — Minimal reconciliation checklist (quick pass)
Use for weekly spot-checks.

3) Sample 10 accounts (2 enterprise, 5 mid-market, 3 self-serve)

```
  product_route,
  price_plan,
  SUM(billed_tokens_total) AS evt_billed_tokens,
  SUM(billable_requests) AS evt_requests,
  SUM(cache_hit_tokens) AS evt_cache_hit_tokens,
  COUNT(*) AS evt_rows
FROM usage_events_deduped
WHERE usage_ts_utc >= :start_ts AND usage_ts_utc < :end_ts
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5,6;
```

**Important:** ensure timezone alignment. All reconciliation is done in **UTC** unless explicitly performing a customer-specific export in their billing timezone.

---

### Step 4 — Pull ledger totals for the same grain
Use the approved ledger reconciliation export (preferred) or ledger table (restricted).

Confirm:
- Ledger includes the same dimension keys (or a documented mapping)
- Price plan changes mid-period are represented consistently

Example (pseudo-SQL):
```
SELECT
  account_id,
  usage_date,
  region,
  model,
  product_route,
  price_plan,
  SUM(billed_tokens_total) AS led_billed_tokens,
  SUM(billable_requests) AS led_requests,
  SUM(amount_usd) AS led_amount_usd
FROM billing_ledger_recon_export
WHERE usage_date BETWEEN :start_date AND :end_date
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5,6;
```

---

### Step 5 — Compute drift and classify discrepancies
Join usage-event aggregates to ledger aggregates and compute:
- Token drift: (evt_billed_tokens - led_billed_tokens)
- Request drift
- Amount drift (if ledger contains pricing)

Classification (pick one primary label):
1) **Late-arriving usage events** (events present after ledger cut; freshness breach)
2) **Duplicate events / idempotency regression** (evt > ledger, duplicates elevated)
3) **Retry/fallback double-count** (streaming + server retry; known failure mode)
4) **Missing dimensions** (region/model/route nulls causing mis-bucketing)
5) **Token definition mismatch** (raw vs billed tokens; tool calls/structured output)
6) **Embeddings batch accounting bug** (per-input vs per-request counting)
7) **Timezone boundary mismatch** (customer export vs ledger day boundaries)
8) **Expected business rule difference** (e.g., free tier, minimums, rounding rules)

Decision rule:
- If drift is **within thresholds** and no systematic bias is observed: record as pass.
- If drift is **outside thresholds**: proceed to Step 6.

---

### Step 6 — Cross-check against customer usage API/export
For customer disputes and customer-visible consistency, compare ledger totals to the customer usage export/API.

Procedure:
1) Pull usage export for same account and UTC period.
2) Confirm export schema version and whether it shows **billed_tokens** vs **raw_tokens**.
3) Reconcile at daily grain, then drill down by model/region/route.

If customer usage export differs from ledger:
- Determine whether it is due to **lag** (export behind ledger) or **definition mismatch**.
- If definition mismatch: document which field is correct for customer messaging and ensure docs/SDK are aligned.

---

### Step 7 — Drill-down playbook by failure mode
Use this section when Step 5 flags drift outside thresholds.

#### A) Late-arriving events
Signals:
- Freshness SLO breach
- Drift concentrated in the most recent 24–48 hours

Actions:
- Re-run reconciliation after pipeline catch-up.
- If ledger has already frozen for the period: do **not** change ledger without CFO/RevRec approval.
- Add incident annotation and attach evidence (lag chart + impacted partitions).

#### B) Duplicate events / idempotency regression
Signals:
- Duplicate rate increases
- Drift correlates with partial failures/backfills

Actions:
- Identify duplicate key collisions and affected endpoints.
- Engineering to confirm dedupe window and idempotency key behavior.
- Prefer forward fix + backfill usage events view; do not retroactively re-issue invoices without approval.

#### C) Retry/fallback double-count (Hosted API)
Signals:
- Drift spikes on streaming/chat
- Higher variance in routes using fallback routing

Actions:
- Validate whether both attempts emitted billable events.
- Confirm request_id/attempt_id relationship in logs.
- If confirmed: implement fix + run shadow billing compare before enabling.

#### D) Missing/incorrect dimensions (region/model/route)
Signals:
- Null dimension rate > threshold
- Drift appears as “missing” in one bucket and “extra” in another

Actions:
- Determine whether ledger applies defaults/mappings that usage events do not.
- Backfill dimension fields if needed (with Finance approval if it changes customer exports).

#### E) Token definition mismatch (raw vs billed)
Signals:
- Customer compares invoice to raw token counts from SDK logs
- Tool calls / structured output present

Actions:
- Confirm invoice uses **billed_tokens** per meter spec.
- Ensure customer usage export includes billed_tokens and is documented.
- If mismatch is due to OpenAI-compatible endpoint semantics: coordinate with Product + Docs.

#### F) Embeddings batch accounting
Signals:
- Drift only on embeddings route
- Customers using batched input arrays

Actions:
- Validate whether per-input tokenization is applied consistently.
- Confirm “billable unit” definition in meter spec.
- Run unit tests / regression scenario where batch sizes vary.

#### G) Timezone boundary mismatch (exports)
Signals:
- Customer sees day-over-day spikes at midnight local time

Actions:
- Confirm invoice period is UTC.
- If customer requires a local-time export, provide it as an informational report but do not change invoice basis unless contract specifies.

---

### Step 8 — Decide remediation path (forward fix vs adjustment)
Use this decision tree once root cause is identified.

**Default policy:** prefer **forward fixes** with clear documentation and audit trail. Avoid retroactive invoice changes.

Remediation options:
1) **No action** (variance within threshold; document)
2) **Forward fix only** (metering bug fixed; applies to future usage)
3) **Customer credit** (invoice remains; credit memo or usage credit issued)
4) **Invoice adjustment / re-issue** (rare; requires CFO + Legal)
5) **Backfill** (usage events/customer export corrected; ledger may or may not change depending on freeze)

Minimum documentation for any remediation:
- Root cause summary
- Impacted period + accounts
- Quantified $ impact (best estimate + bounds)
- Customer comms plan (Support/Sales owner)
- Approvals captured (names + date)

---

### Step 9 — Produce an audit-ready evidence package
Attach to the reconciliation ticket:
- Drift report (by account/day/model/region/route)
- Freshness and completeness charts for the period
- Any incident annotations
- Query fingerprints (table/view names + snapshot timestamp)
- Final sign-off comment (RevRec + Eng)

Retention:
- Store evidence per retention policy; do not export raw request logs.

---

## Appendix A — Minimal reconciliation checklist (quick pass)
Use for weekly spot-checks.
1) Confirm freshness SLO pass
2) Run ledger vs usage events drift (last 7 days)
3) Sample 10 accounts (2 enterprise, 5 mid-market, 3 self-serve)
4) Validate top 5 models per account
5) Confirm null rate and duplicate rate are under thresholds
6) Record pass/fail and open investigations

---

## Appendix B — Customer dispute workflow integration
When Support/Sales opens a billing dispute:
1) Require: invoice id, account id, disputed period, customer’s comparison method (SDK logs vs export)
2) Run Step 6 first (customer usage export alignment)
3) If discrepancy persists, proceed Steps 3–5 at a narrower scope
4) Provide customer-facing explanation using approved language (Legal playbook)
5) If credit is recommended, route for approval per sign-off rules

---

## Appendix C — Known recurring failure modes (historical)
- Streaming retry/fallback double-counting when both attempts emitted billable events
- Missing region dimension leading to mis-priced regional rates and margin attribution errors
- Embeddings batched requests counted per-request instead of per-input tokenization
- Customer export timezone misunderstanding (UTC invoice vs local-time dashboards)

---

## Change control
Any change to metering definitions or ledger derivation logic must follow:
- Shadow billing compare for at least one full business cycle (typically 7 days)
- Documented before/after diffs and expected variance
- Finance/RevRec sign-off prior to enabling invoice-impacting behavior

(See the metering change control process ticket and the meter sanity dashboard spec for alerting requirements.)

source.title: Unit Economics Alignment: Standard Cost Model Outputs → FP&A Reporting (v1)
source.author: Rishi Malhotra
source.content:
## Purpose
This page defines how **Standard Cost Model (SCM)** outputs map to **FP&A unit economics** reporting for Redwood Inference.

It is written for Finance/FP&A, with implementation notes for Platform/Billing/Telemetry teams.

This doc answers:
- What the SCM is (and is not), from a finance perspective.
- Which SCM fields are **finance-reportable** vs **product/observability-only**.
- How to translate SCM outputs into FP&A metrics (COGS, gross margin, contribution margin, per-token unit costs).
- The **overhead allocation policy** for shared infrastructure and how it appears in SCM outputs.
- Reporting windows, cutoffs, backfills, and reconciliation rules.

## Scope and non-goals
### In scope
- Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments where Redwood has cost responsibility.
- GPU compute, CPU/other compute for serving, networking/egress, storage, and shared service overheads that are attributed to serving.
- Alignment of SCM-driven cost attribution with FP&A monthly reporting.

### Not in scope
- Revenue recognition logic (SCM is not the billing or RevRec system of record).
- Customer-facing pricing calculators.
- Allocations unrelated to inference serving (e.g., corporate G&A) unless explicitly listed under "Corporate allocations".

## Definitions
- **Standard Cost Model (SCM):** Versioned specification + internal API producing canonical, comparable cost primitives for inference serving.
- **Predicted cost:** SCM estimate computed from runtime + telemetry inputs, using rates and allocation rules.
- **Observed spend:** Actual cloud/provider invoices (plus internal purchase amortization where applicable), allocated to cost centers.
- **Drift:** Difference between predicted cost and observed spend after applying the reconciliation/true-up methodology.

## Why this alignment matters (Finance perspective)
Historically, Console/Optimize prototypes, telemetry aggregations, and billing hooks have used slightly different cost definitions (e.g., prefill vs decode handling, cache hits, batch efficiency, overhead allocation). That resulted in:
- Unit economics metrics that were hard to reproduce and audit.
- Product dashboards that did not reconcile cleanly to monthly spend.
- Customer escalations when Console breakdowns differed from invoice totals.

SCM establishes a single set of primitives so that:
- FP&A reporting can use consistent categories and stable rollups.
- Console/Optimize can show explainable cost breakdowns.
- Finance can reconcile predicted cost to observed spend with known/controlled differences.

## Canonical SCM cost breakdown taxonomy (finance-relevant)
Finance-relevant outputs are separated into **request-attributable** vs **allocated overhead**.

### Request-attributable cost (direct)
These represent cost that scales with requests/tokens and can be attributed to tenants/routes/models.
- **Compute (GPU):** GPU time attributable to the request, split into:
  - **Prefill stage** (prompt processing)
  - **Decode stage** (token generation)
- **Compute (CPU/aux):** CPU for tokenization, routing, lightweight post-processing (where metered).
- **Network/egress (variable):** Customer egress (if applicable per deployment mode) and inter-AZ transfer when treated as variable.

### Allocated overhead cost (indirect)
These represent shared costs required to run the serving platform and are allocated by policy:
- **Serving platform overhead:** control plane, schedulers, observability pipeline, shared caches that are not request-metered.
- **Idle/fragmentation:** unallocated GPU capacity, fragmentation losses, and reserved headroom for SLOs.
- **Supportable compliance overhead:** required security/compliance infrastructure tied to serving (e.g., audit logging storage) where treated as serving COGS.

## Overhead allocation policy (agreement)
### Policy statement
Overhead is allocated to inference workloads using a two-layer approach:
1) **Pool-level allocation** (by deployment + region + hardware SKU pool) based on time-weighted capacity.
2) **Tenant-level allocation** within a pool based on a stable driver tied to consumption.

### Allocation drivers (v1)
Unless otherwise noted in a specific reporting runbook, the following apply:
- **Hosted API pools:** allocate overhead to tenants by **effective billed tokens** (prefill + decode, net of cache policy as defined below).
- **Dedicated pools:** allocate overhead within a customer’s dedicated pool by **customer traffic share** (generally 100% to that customer), and optionally allocate within their internal projects by effective tokens.
- **Private deployments (Redwood-operated VPC):** allocate overhead by **contracted capacity units** unless metered token attribution is available and validated.

### Cache and batching semantics for allocation drivers
To avoid perverse incentives and to keep FP&A consistent with engineering reality:
- **KV cache hits:**
  - Request-attributable compute should reflect actual GPU work (cache hits reduce compute).
  - Overhead allocation driver should use **effective tokens** defined as:
    - Prefill effective tokens = prefill tokens * (1 - prefill_cache_hit_rate)
    - Decode effective tokens = decode tokens (cache does not apply to decode in the same way)
  - Rationale: overhead allocation should follow actual capacity consumption, not raw user prompt sizes.
- **Batching:** request-attributable GPU cost reflects actual measured/estimated GPU time, capturing batch efficiency improvements. Overhead allocation continues to use effective tokens as the stable driver.

### What is explicitly excluded from overhead pools (v1)
The following are treated as corporate or non-serving and are excluded from SCM overhead allocations:
- Sales & marketing expenses
- Corporate G&A not required to operate serving
- R&D model training (non-inference)

## Mapping SCM outputs to FP&A unit economics
FP&A reporting consumes SCM outputs at two levels:
1) **Finance reportable ledger-level categories** (COGS, infra COGS, etc.)
2) **Product unit economics metrics** (cost per 1K tokens, contribution margin by customer, etc.)

### Mapping table (SCM → FP&A)
| SCM breakdown component | Included in FP&A COGS? | FP&A bucket | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| GPU compute (prefill) | Yes | Infra COGS – GPU | Direct, request-attributable |
| GPU compute (decode) | Yes | Infra COGS – GPU | Direct, request-attributable |
| CPU/aux compute | Yes | Infra COGS – Compute (non-GPU) | Only where metered/validated |
| Network/egress (variable) | Yes | Infra COGS – Network | Subject to provider invoice policy |
| Storage (serving logs/caches) | Yes | Infra COGS – Storage | Only serving-required storage |
| Idle/fragmentation/headroom | Yes | Infra COGS – Capacity overhead | Allocated per pool policy |
| Serving platform overhead | Yes | Infra COGS – Platform overhead | Includes control plane + shared services |
| Incident/credit adjustments | Yes (as adjustment) | COGS adjustments | Managed via true-up process |
| Customer support costs | No (default) | OpEx | Exceptions require Finance approval |

### Standard FP&A metrics and formulas
**Cost per 1K tokens (CP1KT)**
- Numerator: (Direct cost + allocated overhead) in USD
- Denominator: (effective tokens / 1,000)
- Reportable variants:
  - CP1KT – Hosted (by model family)
  - CP1KT – Dedicated (by customer)

**Gross margin (GM)**
- GM = Revenue - (SCM-based COGS after monthly true-up)

**Contribution margin (CM)** (where used internally)
- CM = Revenue - COGS - variable non-COGS costs (e.g., payment processing) per FP&A definitions

## Reporting windows, cutoffs, and backfills
### Reporting cadence
- Daily: SCM-based internal dashboards (Console/Optimize/Finance exploratory).
- Monthly: FP&A close package uses SCM as the primary allocator, with a documented reconciliation to observed spend.

### Time zones and time boundaries
- SCM timestamps are stored in UTC.
- Monthly reporting windows for FP&A follow **calendar month in UTC** unless otherwise specified in the close checklist.

### Data freshness expectations
- Telemetry ingestion and enrichment may lag.
- For FP&A close, SCM outputs are considered final after:
  - Telemetry completeness passes threshold (see below)
  - Provider invoice data for the month is loaded (or estimated with a true-up)

### Completeness and validation thresholds (v1)
For a period to be “finance-reportable” without manual override:
- Token metering coverage: **≥ 99.5%** of requests have complete prefill/decode token counts.
- Runtime metric coverage (cache/batching): **≥ 98%** for pools included in SCM overhead allocation.
- Unattributed spend: **≤ 2.0%** of observed spend remains in "unmapped" after automated allocation.

If thresholds are not met, FP&A may:
- Use last-known-good allocation rates for the impacted pool(s), and
- Document a manual adjustment with owner + rationale.

### Backfills and corrections
- SCM supports backfills when telemetry is late or when instrumentation bugs are fixed.
- Finance reporting follows a **two-pass approach**:
  1) Initial close numbers using SCM snapshot as of close cutoff.
  2) True-up entries in the next period if backfill changes exceed materiality thresholds.

Materiality guideline (internal): if a correction changes monthly serving COGS by **> 1.0%** or by **>$250k** (whichever is smaller), escalate to FP&A leadership for treatment.

## Reconciliation methodology: SCM vs observed spend
### Objective
Ensure SCM-driven COGS rollups reconcile to provider invoices and internal amortization schedules, with clear attribution of differences.

### Monthly reconciliation steps (standard)
1) Pull observed spend by pool (deployment/region/SKU) from Finance source of truth.
2) Pull SCM aggregated predicted cost by the same pool dimensions.
3) Compute drift by pool and overall.
4) Classify drift into:
   - Timing (telemetry lag)
   - Coverage gaps (unmetered traffic)
   - Rate card differences (provider pricing updates)
   - Allocation policy differences (headroom treatment, reserved vs on-demand)
   - Instrumentation bugs
5) Apply an approved true-up factor at the pool level if required for close.

### True-up policy (v1)
- True-up factors are applied at the **pool level** (not per tenant) to preserve explainability.
- True-up is recorded as a separate adjustment line in FP&A to keep SCM primitives stable.
- True-up factors are not fed back into Console/Optimize cost breakdowns without explicit product approval (to avoid confusing end users with finance-only adjustments).

## Versioning, auditability, and retention
### SCM versioning
- SCM outputs are tagged with a **schema version** and **cost model version**.
- FP&A monthly close must record:
  - SCM model version used
  - Allocation policy version
  - Any true-up factors and rationale

### Retention
- Finance-reportable aggregated outputs: minimum **24 months** retention.
- Per-request detailed breakdown: retained per telemetry retention policy; FP&A should not depend on per-request detail for monthly close.

## Roles and ownership
- **FP&A owner (this doc):** Rishi Malhotra
- **Finance approver:** Laura Bennett
- **SCM service owner:** Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park)
- **Telemetry inputs owner:** Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright)
- **Billing hooks integration:** Cole Summers

Escalation path for close-impacting issues:
1) FP&A (Rishi) + SCM oncall (Platform)
2) Platform Eng manager + Telemetry lead
3) CFO/FP&A leadership + Platform exec sponsor

## FAQ
### Does SCM need to match invoices exactly?
No. SCM is an attribution and unit economics model. FP&A uses SCM as the allocator, then reconciles to invoices using documented drift classification and true-ups.

### Will Console show the same numbers as FP&A reports?
Console should show SCM breakdowns computed from engineering primitives. FP&A may apply month-end true-ups that are finance-only adjustments and may not be exposed in Console.

### How are Dedicated contracts handled?
Dedicated is primarily capacity-based. SCM still estimates direct cost and overhead attribution for internal economics, but FP&A revenue recognition and pricing terms are handled outside SCM.

### What happens when the model or allocation policy changes mid-month?
SCM versioning records the change. FP&A monthly close uses the SCM snapshot and may choose to lock to a version for the reporting window or apply a segmented approach if required for auditability.

## Appendix A: Practical example (illustrative)
Assume Hosted pool “us-east / H100” for February:
- Observed spend: $5,000,000
- SCM predicted (direct + overhead): $4,900,000
- Drift: +$100,000 (2.04%)

Classification:
- $60,000 timing (telemetry lag)
- $40,000 rate card mismatch (provider price update)

FP&A action:
- Apply pool-level true-up factor = 5,000,000 / 4,900,000 = 1.020408
- Record $100,000 as COGS adjustment line with notes

## Appendix B: Reporting checklist (month-end)
- [ ] Confirm SCM model version and allocation policy version for the month
- [ ] Verify completeness thresholds met or document exceptions
- [ ] Run pool-level reconciliation and classify drift
- [ ] Apply and document any true-up factors
- [ ] Export FP&A tables: COGS by deployment mode, region, SKU; unit costs per 1K tokens
- [ ] Archive artifacts (versions, queries, dashboards, and approvals)

  1) Initial close numbers using SCM snapshot as of close cutoff.
  2) True-up entries in the next period if backfill changes exceed materiality thresholds.

Materiality guideline (internal): if a correction changes monthly serving COGS by **> 1.0%** or by **>$250k** (whichever is smaller), escalate to FP&A leadership for treatment.

## Reconciliation methodology: SCM vs observed spend
### Objective
Ensure SCM-driven COGS rollups reconcile to provider invoices and internal amortization schedules, with clear attribution of differences.

### Monthly reconciliation steps (standard)
1) Pull observed spend by pool (deployment/region/SKU) from Finance source of truth.
2) Pull SCM aggregated predicted cost by the same pool dimensions.
3) Compute drift by pool and overall.
4) Classify drift into:
   - Timing (telemetry lag)
   - Coverage gaps (unmetered traffic)
   - Rate card differences (provider pricing updates)
   - Allocation policy differences (headroom treatment, reserved vs on-demand)
   - Instrumentation bugs
5) Apply an approved true-up factor at the pool level if required for close.

### True-up policy (v1)
- True-up factors are applied at the **pool level** (not per tenant) to preserve explainability.
- True-up is recorded as a separate adjustment line in FP&A to keep SCM primitives stable.
- True-up factors are not fed back into Console/Optimize cost breakdowns without explicit product approval (to avoid confusing end users with finance-only adjustments).

## Versioning, auditability, and retention
### SCM versioning
- SCM outputs are tagged with a **schema version** and **cost model version**.
- FP&A monthly close must record:
  - SCM model version used
  - Allocation policy version
  - Any true-up factors and rationale

### Retention
- Finance-reportable aggregated outputs: minimum **24 months** retention.
- Per-request detailed breakdown: retained per telemetry retention policy; FP&A should not depend on per-request detail for monthly close.

## Roles and ownership
- **FP&A owner (this doc):** Rishi Malhotra
- **Finance approver:** Laura Bennett
- **SCM service owner:** Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park)
- **Telemetry inputs owner:** Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright)
- **Billing hooks integration:** Cole Summers

Escalation path for close-impacting issues:
1) FP&A (Rishi) + SCM oncall (Platform)
2) Platform Eng manager + Telemetry lead
3) CFO/FP&A leadership + Platform exec sponsor

## FAQ
### Does SCM need to match invoices exactly?
No. SCM is an attribution and unit economics model. FP&A uses SCM as the allocator, then reconciles to invoices using documented drift classification and true-ups.

### Will Console show the same numbers as FP&A reports?
Console should show SCM breakdowns computed from engineering primitives. FP&A may apply month-end true-ups that are finance-only adjustments and may not be exposed in Console.

### How are Dedicated contracts handled?
Dedicated is primarily capacity-based. SCM still estimates direct cost and overhead attribution for internal economics, but FP&A revenue recognition and pricing terms are handled outside SCM.

### What happens when the model or allocation policy changes mid-month?
SCM versioning records the change. FP&A monthly close uses the SCM snapshot and may choose to lock to a version for the reporting window or apply a segmented approach if required for auditability.

## Appendix A: Practical example (illustrative)
Assume Hosted pool “us-east / H100” for February:
- Observed spend: $5,000,000
- SCM predicted (direct + overhead): $4,900,000
- Drift: +$100,000 (2.04%)

Classification:
- $60,000 timing (telemetry lag)
- $40,000 rate card mismatch (provider price update)

FP&A action:
- Apply pool-level true-up factor = 5,000,000 / 4,900,000 = 1.020408
- Record $100,000 as COGS adjustment line with notes

## Appendix B: Reporting checklist (month-end)
- [ ] Confirm SCM model version and allocation policy version for the month
- [ ] Verify completeness thresholds met or document exceptions
- [ ] Run pool-level reconciliation and classify drift
- [ ] Apply and document any true-up factors
- [ ] Export FP&A tables: COGS by deployment mode, region, SKU; unit costs per 1K tokens
- [ ] Archive artifacts (versions, queries, dashboards, and approvals)
text:
## Purpose
This page defines how **Standard Cost Model (SCM)** outputs map to **FP&A unit economics** reporting for Redwood Inference.

It is written for Finance/FP&A, with implementation notes for Platform/Billing/Telemetry teams.

This doc answers:
- What the SCM is (and is not), from a finance perspective.
- Which SCM fields are **finance-reportable** vs **product/observability-only**.
- How to translate SCM outputs into FP&A metrics (COGS, gross margin, contribution margin, per-token unit costs).
- The **overhead allocation policy** for shared infrastructure and how it appears in SCM outputs.
- Reporting windows, cutoffs, backfills, and reconciliation rules.

## Scope and non-goals
### In scope
- Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments where Redwood has cost responsibility.
- GPU compute, CPU/other compute for serving, networking/egress, storage, and shared service overheads that are attributed to serving.
- Alignment of SCM-driven cost attribution with FP&A monthly reporting.

### Not in scope
- Revenue recognition logic (SCM is not the billing or RevRec system of record).
- Customer-facing pricing calculators.
- Allocations unrelated to inference serving (e.g., corporate G&A) unless explicitly listed under "Corporate allocations".

## Definitions
- **Standard Cost Model (SCM):** Versioned specification + internal API producing canonical, comparable cost primitives for inference serving.
- **Predicted cost:** SCM estimate computed from runtime + telemetry inputs, using rates and allocation rules.
- **Observed spend:** Actual cloud/provider invoices (plus internal purchase amortization where applicable), allocated to cost centers.
- **Drift:** Difference between predicted cost and observed spend after applying the reconciliation/true-up methodology.

## Why this alignment matters (Finance perspective)
Historically, Console/Optimize prototypes, telemetry aggregations, and billing hooks have used slightly different cost definitions (e.g., prefill vs decode handling, cache hits, batch efficiency, overhead allocation). That resulted in:
- Unit economics metrics that were hard to reproduce and audit.
- Product dashboards that did not reconcile cleanly to monthly spend.
- Customer escalations when Console breakdowns differed from invoice totals.

SCM establishes a single set of primitives so that:
- FP&A reporting can use consistent categories and stable rollups.
- Console/Optimize can show explainable cost breakdowns.
- Finance can reconcile predicted cost to observed spend with known/controlled differences.

## Canonical SCM cost breakdown taxonomy (finance-relevant)
Finance-relevant outputs are separated into **request-attributable** vs **allocated overhead**.

### Request-attributable cost (direct)
These represent cost that scales with requests/tokens and can be attributed to tenants/routes/models.
- **Compute (GPU):** GPU time attributable to the request, split into:
  - **Prefill stage** (prompt processing)
  - **Decode stage** (token generation)
- **Compute (CPU/aux):** CPU for tokenization, routing, lightweight post-processing (where metered).
- **Network/egress (variable):** Customer egress (if applicable per deployment mode) and inter-AZ transfer when treated as variable.

### Allocated overhead cost (indirect)
These represent shared costs required to run the serving platform and are allocated by policy:
- **Serving platform overhead:** control plane, schedulers, observability pipeline, shared caches that are not request-metered.
- **Idle/fragmentation:** unallocated GPU capacity, fragmentation losses, and reserved headroom for SLOs.
- **Supportable compliance overhead:** required security/compliance infrastructure tied to serving (e.g., audit logging storage) where treated as serving COGS.

## Overhead allocation policy (agreement)
### Policy statement
Overhead is allocated to inference workloads using a two-layer approach:
1) **Pool-level allocation** (by deployment + region + hardware SKU pool) based on time-weighted capacity.
2) **Tenant-level allocation** within a pool based on a stable driver tied to consumption.

### Allocation drivers (v1)
Unless otherwise noted in a specific reporting runbook, the following apply:
- **Hosted API pools:** allocate overhead to tenants by **effective billed tokens** (prefill + decode, net of cache policy as defined below).
- **Dedicated pools:** allocate overhead within a customer’s dedicated pool by **customer traffic share** (generally 100% to that customer), and optionally allocate within their internal projects by effective tokens.
- **Private deployments (Redwood-operated VPC):** allocate overhead by **contracted capacity units** unless metered token attribution is available and validated.

### Cache and batching semantics for allocation drivers
To avoid perverse incentives and to keep FP&A consistent with engineering reality:
- **KV cache hits:**
  - Request-attributable compute should reflect actual GPU work (cache hits reduce compute).
  - Overhead allocation driver should use **effective tokens** defined as:
    - Prefill effective tokens = prefill tokens * (1 - prefill_cache_hit_rate)
    - Decode effective tokens = decode tokens (cache does not apply to decode in the same way)
  - Rationale: overhead allocation should follow actual capacity consumption, not raw user prompt sizes.
- **Batching:** request-attributable GPU cost reflects actual measured/estimated GPU time, capturing batch efficiency improvements. Overhead allocation continues to use effective tokens as the stable driver.

### What is explicitly excluded from overhead pools (v1)
The following are treated as corporate or non-serving and are excluded from SCM overhead allocations:
- Sales & marketing expenses
- Corporate G&A not required to operate serving
- R&D model training (non-inference)

## Mapping SCM outputs to FP&A unit economics
FP&A reporting consumes SCM outputs at two levels:
1) **Finance reportable ledger-level categories** (COGS, infra COGS, etc.)
2) **Product unit economics metrics** (cost per 1K tokens, contribution margin by customer, etc.)

### Mapping table (SCM → FP&A)
| SCM breakdown component | Included in FP&A COGS? | FP&A bucket | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| GPU compute (prefill) | Yes | Infra COGS – GPU | Direct, request-attributable |
| GPU compute (decode) | Yes | Infra COGS – GPU | Direct, request-attributable |
| CPU/aux compute | Yes | Infra COGS – Compute (non-GPU) | Only where metered/validated |
| Network/egress (variable) | Yes | Infra COGS – Network | Subject to provider invoice policy |
| Storage (serving logs/caches) | Yes | Infra COGS – Storage | Only serving-required storage |
| Idle/fragmentation/headroom | Yes | Infra COGS – Capacity overhead | Allocated per pool policy |
| Serving platform overhead | Yes | Infra COGS – Platform overhead | Includes control plane + shared services |
| Incident/credit adjustments | Yes (as adjustment) | COGS adjustments | Managed via true-up process |
| Customer support costs | No (default) | OpEx | Exceptions require Finance approval |

### Standard FP&A metrics and formulas
**Cost per 1K tokens (CP1KT)**
- Numerator: (Direct cost + allocated overhead) in USD
- Denominator: (effective tokens / 1,000)
- Reportable variants:
  - CP1KT – Hosted (by model family)
  - CP1KT – Dedicated (by customer)

**Gross margin (GM)**
- GM = Revenue - (SCM-based COGS after monthly true-up)

**Contribution margin (CM)** (where used internally)
- CM = Revenue - COGS - variable non-COGS costs (e.g., payment processing) per FP&A definitions

## Reporting windows, cutoffs, and backfills
### Reporting cadence
- Daily: SCM-based internal dashboards (Console/Optimize/Finance exploratory).
- Monthly: FP&A close package uses SCM as the primary allocator, with a documented reconciliation to observed spend.

### Time zones and time boundaries
- SCM timestamps are stored in UTC.
- Monthly reporting windows for FP&A follow **calendar month in UTC** unless otherwise specified in the close checklist.

### Data freshness expectations
- Telemetry ingestion and enrichment may lag.
- For FP&A close, SCM outputs are considered final after:
  - Telemetry completeness passes threshold (see below)
  - Provider invoice data for the month is loaded (or estimated with a true-up)

### Completeness and validation thresholds (v1)
For a period to be “finance-reportable” without manual override:
- Token metering coverage: **≥ 99.5%** of requests have complete prefill/decode token counts.
- Runtime metric coverage (cache/batching): **≥ 98%** for pools included in SCM overhead allocation.
- Unattributed spend: **≤ 2.0%** of observed spend remains in "unmapped" after automated allocation.

If thresholds are not met, FP&A may:
- Use last-known-good allocation rates for the impacted pool(s), and
- Document a manual adjustment with owner + rationale.

### Backfills and corrections
- SCM supports backfills when telemetry is late or when instrumentation bugs are fixed.
- Finance reporting follows a **two-pass approach**:
  1) Initial close numbers using SCM snapshot as of close cutoff.
  2) True-up entries in the next period if backfill changes exceed materiality thresholds.

Materiality guideline (internal): if a correction changes monthly serving COGS by **> 1.0%** or by **>$250k** (whichever is smaller), escalate to FP&A leadership for treatment.

## Reconciliation methodology: SCM vs observed spend
### Objective
Ensure SCM-driven COGS rollups reconcile to provider invoices and internal amortization schedules, with clear attribution of differences.

### Monthly reconciliation steps (standard)
1) Pull observed spend by pool (deployment/region/SKU) from Finance source of truth.
2) Pull SCM aggregated predicted cost by the same pool dimensions.
3) Compute drift by pool and overall.
4) Classify drift into:
   - Timing (telemetry lag)
   - Coverage gaps (unmetered traffic)
   - Rate card differences (provider pricing updates)
   - Allocation policy differences (headroom treatment, reserved vs on-demand)
   - Instrumentation bugs
5) Apply an approved true-up factor at the pool level if required for close.

### True-up policy (v1)
- True-up factors are applied at the **pool level** (not per tenant) to preserve explainability.
- True-up is recorded as a separate adjustment line in FP&A to keep SCM primitives stable.
- True-up factors are not fed back into Console/Optimize cost breakdowns without explicit product approval (to avoid confusing end users with finance-only adjustments).

## Versioning, auditability, and retention
### SCM versioning
- SCM outputs are tagged with a **schema version** and **cost model version**.
- FP&A monthly close must record:
  - SCM model version used
  - Allocation policy version
  - Any true-up factors and rationale

### Retention
- Finance-reportable aggregated outputs: minimum **24 months** retention.
- Per-request detailed breakdown: retained per telemetry retention policy; FP&A should not depend on per-request detail for monthly close.

## Roles and ownership
- **FP&A owner (this doc):** Rishi Malhotra
- **Finance approver:** Laura Bennett
- **SCM service owner:** Platform Control Plane (Ethan Park)
- **Telemetry inputs owner:** Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright)
- **Billing hooks integration:** Cole Summers

Escalation path for close-impacting issues:
1) FP&A (Rishi) + SCM oncall (Platform)
2) Platform Eng manager + Telemetry lead
3) CFO/FP&A leadership + Platform exec sponsor

## FAQ
### Does SCM need to match invoices exactly?
No. SCM is an attribution and unit economics model. FP&A uses SCM as the allocator, then reconciles to invoices using documented drift classification and true-ups.

### Will Console show the same numbers as FP&A reports?
Console should show SCM breakdowns computed from engineering primitives. FP&A may apply month-end true-ups that are finance-only adjustments and may not be exposed in Console.

### How are Dedicated contracts handled?
Dedicated is primarily capacity-based. SCM still estimates direct cost and overhead attribution for internal economics, but FP&A revenue recognition and pricing terms are handled outside SCM.

### What happens when the model or allocation policy changes mid-month?
SCM versioning records the change. FP&A monthly close uses the SCM snapshot and may choose to lock to a version for the reporting window or apply a segmented approach if required for auditability.

## Appendix A: Practical example (illustrative)
Assume Hosted pool “us-east / H100” for February:
- Observed spend: $5,000,000
- SCM predicted (direct + overhead): $4,900,000
- Drift: +$100,000 (2.04%)

Classification:
- $60,000 timing (telemetry lag)
- $40,000 rate card mismatch (provider price update)

FP&A action:
- Apply pool-level true-up factor = 5,000,000 / 4,900,000 = 1.020408
- Record $100,000 as COGS adjustment line with notes

## Appendix B: Reporting checklist (month-end)
- [ ] Confirm SCM model version and allocation policy version for the month
- [ ] Verify completeness thresholds met or document exceptions
- [ ] Run pool-level reconciliation and classify drift
- [ ] Apply and document any true-up factors
- [ ] Export FP&A tables: COGS by deployment mode, region, SKU; unit costs per 1K tokens
- [ ] Archive artifacts (versions, queries, dashboards, and approvals)

source.title: Forecast-Aligned Purchasing Authorization Framework
source.author: Rachel Meyer
source.body:
Summary
------
This document defines the Forecast-Aligned Purchasing Authorization Framework (FAPAF): a single, auditable process that ties purchase approvals to active budget forecasts and anticipated revenue recognition timelines. The goal is to reduce off-budget spend, improve procurement predictability, and ensure finance has timely inputs for RevRec and billing handoffs.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all Redwood Inference employees and contractors initiating purchase requests (POs, contracts, reserved capacity, and subscriptions).
- Covers capital (CapEx) and operating (OpEx) purchases that affect monthly/annual forecasts or revenue recognition timelines.
- Integrates Budget Owners, Procurement, Legal, and Revenue Operations (RevOps).

Key principles
--------------
1. Forecast-first approvals: every purchase > $10k USD or that creates a multi-period obligation must reference a forecast line and variance rationale.
2. Approval ceiling tiers are aligned to forecast buckets and owner responsibilities (see Approval Matrix).
3. RevRec-aware commitments: any purchase that creates customer-facing billing or deferred revenue consequences must include RevRec tagging and an expected recognition schedule.
4. Single source of truth: all approvals occur through Redwood Finance Portal (RFP) or integrated procurement system and must include a forecast ID.

Approval matrix (summary)
------------------------
- Tier 0: < $2,500 — Manager approval (no forecast match required).
- Tier 1: $2,500–$25,000 — Department Head approval + budget line reference.
- Tier 2: $25,001–$250,000 — Budget Owner + Procurement + Legal (if contract) approvals + forecast variance note.
- Tier 3: > $250,000 or multi-year commitment — CFO approval + Procurement Director + Legal + RevOps review.

(For international purchases convert to USD at the finance daily FX rate and use same thresholds.)

Purchase lifecycle (step-by-step)
--------------------------------
1. Request initiation: Requestor files a Purchase Request (PR) in RFP with a short description, vendor, amount, supplier TIN, forecast ID, forecast impact (one-line), and requested delivery/term dates.
2. Automated validation: RFP checks forecast ID validity and existing commitments for the forecast line. If forecast capacity is insufficient, the PR is routed to Budget Owner for an uplift/exception.
3. Approvals: Required approvers are determined by the Approval Matrix. Legal is auto-requested for any contract or SOW.
4. Procurement execution: Procurement issues PO / negotiates contract and records supplier metadata.
5. RevRec tagging & billing handoff: For purchases that affect billing or deferred revenue (for example, customer pass-throughs, third-party services billed to customers), RevOps assigns RevRec tags (revenue category, recognition start, recognition pattern) and creates the RevRec ticket.
6. Close & record: Finalized PO/contract attached to PR, finance records commitment for forecast consumption and posts to commitments ledger.

Roles and responsibilities
--------------------------
- Requestor: completes PR accurately; provides justification and forecast ID.
- Manager / Department Head: confirms business need and policy compliance.
- Budget Owner: verifies forecast alignment and approves or requests uplift.
- Procurement: negotiates terms, issues PO, maintains vendor onboarding checklist.
- Legal: reviews contracts, ensures clause templates and data-residency/KMS requirements are satisfied.
- RevOps / Finance: tags commitments for RevRec, reconciles forecast consumption weekly, and updates billing teams.

Forecast integration rules (operational detail)
----------------------------------------------
- Every PR must reference an active forecast line (forecast ID). If no appropriate line exists, Budget Owner creates a forecast movement request with expected GL mapping and submits to Finance within 3 business days.
- Commitments reduce available forecast capacity as soon as PR is approved by Procurement. This ensures downstream teams see true available headroom.
- Monthly cutoffs: PRs approved after the 20th of a month but with vendor start dates in the next month must be included in the next month’s forecast update package.

Revenue recognition (RevRec) and billing impact
------------------------------------------------
- When purchases create deferred revenue or influence customer billing (e.g., third-party service resold to customers, prepaid support included with client contracts), RevOps must be looped in during the procurement review.
- RevRec tagging fields required on the PR: "revrec_category", "recognition_start_date", "recognition_pattern" (e.g., immediate, monthly_pro_rata, straight_line). Example: 

```
{
  "revrec_category": "prepaid_support",
  "recognition_start_date": "2026-04-01",
  "recognition_pattern": "monthly_pro_rata"
}
```

System configuration and automation notes
----------------------------------------
- RFP must enforce forecast ID presence via a mandatory field and reject PRs without it.
- Integrations: RFP <-> Procurement System (PO issuance), RFP <-> RevRec system (ticket creation and tag sync), RFP <-> ERP (commitment posting).
- Webhook example (Procurement -> RevOps) payload: use JSON snippet above with PR id and PO id.

Exceptional approvals and off-cycle requests
------------------------------------------
- Off-cycle purchases (urgent security, compliance, or production hotfixes) may be approved by the CTO and CFO jointly up to $500k; Procurement and Legal follow-up must occur within 5 business days.
- Emergency spend must still be logged into RFP with an "emergency" flag and retroactive forecast alignment submitted within the same fiscal quarter.

Control and auditability
-----------------------
- Every PR/PO/contract must contain the forecast ID and the approval path; missing metadata is a policy violation and will be flagged by internal audit.
- Finance runs weekly commitment reports and a monthly reconciled commitments-to-forecast report; exceptions are escalated to Finance Leadership.
- Retention: procurement records and RevRec tags are retained for 7 years per finance policy.

Implementation roadmap
----------------------
1. Q1 2025: Policy ratification + RFP adjustments to require forecast ID and add RevRec fields.
2. Q2 2025: Procurement systems integration (PO automation) and Legal template updates.
3. Q3 2025: RevOps integration and end-to-end testing using three pilot teams (Infrastructure, Product, Sales Ops).
4. Q4 2025: Full company rollout and training.

Helpful reference links
-----------------------
- Contract templates: confluence://finance-and-legal/contracts-and-legal-process/contract-clauses-escrow-kms-data-residency-2026
- Monthly forecast cadence: confluence://finance-and-legal/budgeting-and-forecasting/monthly-forecast-process-2025
- RFP user guide (Procurement system): confluence://eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/rfp-integration-guide

Appendices
----------
A. Sample PR summary (text to paste into RFP)
- Title: Reserved GPU capacity for Q3 campaign (3-month burst)
- Vendor: RedCloud Compute, Inc.
- Amount: $72,000 USD; multi-month prepaid
- Forecast ID: INFRA_Q3_2025_BURST
- RevRec Category: prepaid_infrastructure
- Recognition: straight_line from 2025-07-01 to 2025-09-30

B. Commonly used GL mappings
- CapEx: 6000-Equipment
- OpEx – Cloud compute: 6200-CloudServices
- Professional services: 6400-Consulting

Questions and feedback
----------------------
For questions or to request an exception, contact Procurement (procurement@redwood.com) or Finance (finance-ops@redwood.com).
