A. Sample PR summary (text to paste into RFP)
- Title: Reserved GPU capacity for Q3 campaign (3-month burst)
- Vendor: RedCloud Compute, Inc.
- Amount: $72,000 USD; multi-month prepaid
- Forecast ID: INFRA_Q3_2025_BURST
- RevRec Category: prepaid_infrastructure
- Recognition: straight_line from 2025-07-01 to 2025-09-30

B. Commonly used GL mappings
- CapEx: 6000-Equipment
- OpEx – Cloud compute: 6200-CloudServices
- Professional services: 6400-Consulting

Questions and feedback
----------------------
For questions or to request an exception, contact Procurement (procurement@redwood.com) or Finance (finance-ops@redwood.com).
text:
Summary
------
This document defines the Forecast-Aligned Purchasing Authorization Framework (FAPAF): a single, auditable process that ties purchase approvals to active budget forecasts and anticipated revenue recognition timelines. The goal is to reduce off-budget spend, improve procurement predictability, and ensure finance has timely inputs for RevRec and billing handoffs.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all Redwood Inference employees and contractors initiating purchase requests (POs, contracts, reserved capacity, and subscriptions).
- Covers capital (CapEx) and operating (OpEx) purchases that affect monthly/annual forecasts or revenue recognition timelines.
- Integrates Budget Owners, Procurement, Legal, and Revenue Operations (RevOps).

Key principles
--------------
1. Forecast-first approvals: every purchase > $10k USD or that creates a multi-period obligation must reference a forecast line and variance rationale.
2. Approval ceiling tiers are aligned to forecast buckets and owner responsibilities (see Approval Matrix).
3. RevRec-aware commitments: any purchase that creates customer-facing billing or deferred revenue consequences must include RevRec tagging and an expected recognition schedule.
4. Single source of truth: all approvals occur through Redwood Finance Portal (RFP) or integrated procurement system and must include a forecast ID.

Approval matrix (summary)
------------------------
- Tier 0: < $2,500 — Manager approval (no forecast match required).
- Tier 1: $2,500–$25,000 — Department Head approval + budget line reference.
- Tier 2: $25,001–$250,000 — Budget Owner + Procurement + Legal (if contract) approvals + forecast variance note.
- Tier 3: > $250,000 or multi-year commitment — CFO approval + Procurement Director + Legal + RevOps review.

(For international purchases convert to USD at the finance daily FX rate and use same thresholds.)

Purchase lifecycle (step-by-step)
--------------------------------
1. Request initiation: Requestor files a Purchase Request (PR) in RFP with a short description, vendor, amount, supplier TIN, forecast ID, forecast impact (one-line), and requested delivery/term dates.
2. Automated validation: RFP checks forecast ID validity and existing commitments for the forecast line. If forecast capacity is insufficient, the PR is routed to Budget Owner for an uplift/exception.
3. Approvals: Required approvers are determined by the Approval Matrix. Legal is auto-requested for any contract or SOW.
4. Procurement execution: Procurement issues PO / negotiates contract and records supplier metadata.
5. RevRec tagging & billing handoff: For purchases that affect billing or deferred revenue (for example, customer pass-throughs, third-party services billed to customers), RevOps assigns RevRec tags (revenue category, recognition start, recognition pattern) and creates the RevRec ticket.
6. Close & record: Finalized PO/contract attached to PR, finance records commitment for forecast consumption and posts to commitments ledger.

Roles and responsibilities
--------------------------
- Requestor: completes PR accurately; provides justification and forecast ID.
- Manager / Department Head: confirms business need and policy compliance.
- Budget Owner: verifies forecast alignment and approves or requests uplift.
- Procurement: negotiates terms, issues PO, maintains vendor onboarding checklist.
- Legal: reviews contracts, ensures clause templates and data-residency/KMS requirements are satisfied.
- RevOps / Finance: tags commitments for RevRec, reconciles forecast consumption weekly, and updates billing teams.

Forecast integration rules (operational detail)
----------------------------------------------
- Every PR must reference an active forecast line (forecast ID). If no appropriate line exists, Budget Owner creates a forecast movement request with expected GL mapping and submits to Finance within 3 business days.
- Commitments reduce available forecast capacity as soon as PR is approved by Procurement. This ensures downstream teams see true available headroom.
- Monthly cutoffs: PRs approved after the 20th of a month but with vendor start dates in the next month must be included in the next month’s forecast update package.

Revenue recognition (RevRec) and billing impact
------------------------------------------------
- When purchases create deferred revenue or influence customer billing (e.g., third-party service resold to customers, prepaid support included with client contracts), RevOps must be looped in during the procurement review.
- RevRec tagging fields required on the PR: "revrec_category", "recognition_start_date", "recognition_pattern" (e.g., immediate, monthly_pro_rata, straight_line). Example: 

```
{
  "revrec_category": "prepaid_support",
  "recognition_start_date": "2026-04-01",
  "recognition_pattern": "monthly_pro_rata"
}
```

System configuration and automation notes
----------------------------------------
- RFP must enforce forecast ID presence via a mandatory field and reject PRs without it.
- Integrations: RFP <-> Procurement System (PO issuance), RFP <-> RevRec system (ticket creation and tag sync), RFP <-> ERP (commitment posting).
- Webhook example (Procurement -> RevOps) payload: use JSON snippet above with PR id and PO id.

Exceptional approvals and off-cycle requests
------------------------------------------
- Off-cycle purchases (urgent security, compliance, or production hotfixes) may be approved by the CTO and CFO jointly up to $500k; Procurement and Legal follow-up must occur within 5 business days.
- Emergency spend must still be logged into RFP with an "emergency" flag and retroactive forecast alignment submitted within the same fiscal quarter.

Control and auditability
-----------------------
- Every PR/PO/contract must contain the forecast ID and the approval path; missing metadata is a policy violation and will be flagged by internal audit.
- Finance runs weekly commitment reports and a monthly reconciled commitments-to-forecast report; exceptions are escalated to Finance Leadership.
- Retention: procurement records and RevRec tags are retained for 7 years per finance policy.

Implementation roadmap
----------------------
1. Q1 2025: Policy ratification + RFP adjustments to require forecast ID and add RevRec fields.
2. Q2 2025: Procurement systems integration (PO automation) and Legal template updates.
3. Q3 2025: RevOps integration and end-to-end testing using three pilot teams (Infrastructure, Product, Sales Ops).
4. Q4 2025: Full company rollout and training.

Helpful reference links
-----------------------
- Contract templates: confluence://finance-and-legal/contracts-and-legal-process/contract-clauses-escrow-kms-data-residency-2026
- Monthly forecast cadence: confluence://finance-and-legal/budgeting-and-forecasting/monthly-forecast-process-2025
- RFP user guide (Procurement system): confluence://eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/rfp-integration-guide

Appendices
----------
A. Sample PR summary (text to paste into RFP)
- Title: Reserved GPU capacity for Q3 campaign (3-month burst)
- Vendor: RedCloud Compute, Inc.
- Amount: $72,000 USD; multi-month prepaid
- Forecast ID: INFRA_Q3_2025_BURST
- RevRec Category: prepaid_infrastructure
- Recognition: straight_line from 2025-07-01 to 2025-09-30

B. Commonly used GL mappings
- CapEx: 6000-Equipment
- OpEx – Cloud compute: 6200-CloudServices
- Professional services: 6400-Consulting

Questions and feedback
----------------------
For questions or to request an exception, contact Procurement (procurement@redwood.com) or Finance (finance-ops@redwood.com).

source.title: Procurement Approval Matrix and Billing-Trigger Guide
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Purpose
-------
This page defines Redwood Inference's standardized procurement approval matrix, the lifecycle steps for contract execution, and the precise billing triggers that feed the revenue recognition (revrec) process. It is intended for Finance, Legal, Procurement owners, and Engineering or Product teams who initiate supplier commitments. The goal is to reduce cycle time, ensure correct accounting treatment at go-live, and avoid revenue/billing misalignment across hosted, dedicated, and private product lines.

Scope
-----
Applies to: all third-party software and hardware purchases, cloud/hosted commitments, professional services, and vendor contracts that create a legal obligation or financial commitment for Redwood Inference (including pilot agreements and proof-of-concept statements of work).
Excludes: petty cash, employee expense reimbursements, and subscriptions procured on employee cards under $500 (see petty-expense policy).

Key Definitions
---------------
- Commitment: any signed agreement or PO that obligates Redwood to pay or receive services.
- Billing Trigger: the event which causes the vendor to generate invoices and which typically corresponds to the point at which Redwood recognizes expense or revenue impact.
- Effective Date vs. Service Commencement: Effective Date is when the contract is executed; Service Commencement is when vendor obligations start (these can be different).

Summary of Approval Matrix
--------------------------
Approval thresholds are financial (total contract value over term) and categorical (SaaS, infra, professional services, capital equipment). Routing times assume standard 5 business day SLA for each approver unless flagged. Use the Procurement Portal to initiate all requests.

Table: Approval Matrix (text tabular view)
Value band / Category | Requestor -> Approvers (order) | Required Attachments | Typical SLA
---------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------|---------
<$25k (non-capex) | Requesting Manager -> Procurement -> Finance (inform) | Quote, business justification | 3–7 days
$25k–$150k | Requesting Manager -> Procurement -> Finance -> Legal (if T&C change) -> Procurement Head | Quote, ROI or cost-center approval, security checklist | 7–12 days
$150k–$750k | Requesting Director -> Procurement -> Finance -> Legal -> Head of Product/Eng -> CFO (if multiyear) | Contract redline, SOC/attestation, procurement risk assessment | 10–20 days
>$750k or capital equipment | VP-level sponsor -> Procurement Director -> Legal -> CFO -> CEO (if > $2M/3yr) | Full diligence pack, board notice (if required) | 15–45 days
Special categories: any contract involving customer data residency, on-prem deployment, or KMS/HSM access must route to Security & Compliance prior to Legal review (add 5 business days).

Roles & Responsibilities
------------------------
- Requesting Manager: complete procurement request in portal, attach justification, and define cost-center and budget tag.
- Procurement: primary owner of vendor selection, PO issuance, and supplier onboarding checklist.
- Finance: verifies budget availability, assigns GL codes, and confirms revrec/billing implications.
- Legal: reviews and negotiates T&Cs; signs if within delegated authority.
- Security & Compliance: performs data-security risk review for relevant categories.
- Contracts Admin: executes signature workflows and uploads countersigned agreement to contract registry.

Standard Contract Lifecycle (5-step process)
-------------------------------------------
1) Intake & Business Justification
   - Requestor opens procurement ticket (Portal -> New Request) and attaches: vendor quote, scope of work, cost-center approval, and expected go-live date.
2) Risk & Compliance Screening (automatic)
   - Procurement auto-runs vendor risk checks (sanctions, AML, basic security posture). If flagged, Security & Compliance engages.
3) Negotiation & Approvals
   - Legal negotiates commercial terms and redlines. Approvers are notified in the order defined by the Approval Matrix.
4) Signature & PO Issuance
   - Contracts Admin finalizes signature (DocuSign). Procurement issues PO referencing contract ID and expected billing trigger.
5) Onboarding & Accounting Setup
   - Vendor is added to AP, GL mapping applied, invoicing instructions recorded, and revrec treatment confirmed in the contract registry.

Billing Triggers and RevRec Alignment
------------------------------------
Each contract requires an explicit Billing Trigger field to be set at signature. Typical triggers and how Finance treats them for expense recognition and billing purposes:
- Invoice on execution: vendor invoices after signature. Finance posts expense when invoice received; revrec not applicable (vendor expense only).
- Monthly in arrears based on usage: vendor invoices monthly for actual usage. Finance accrues monthly; for Redwood customer-facing revenue (if vendor pass-through), match usage reports to invoiced amounts.
- Milestone-based invoicing: vendor invoices on achievement of defined milestones. Finance recognizes expense as milestones are met; procurements that create customer billable services must map vendor milestones to customer billing milestones in product contracts to avoid mismatched revenue timing.
- Delivery + Acceptance: hardware or on-prem appliances invoiced on delivery and customer acceptance. Capitalization policy applies for items meeting fixed-asset thresholds (see capitalization policy).
- Supply or Capacity Reservation (committed capacity): vendor invoices per agreed commitment schedule even if unused. Finance treats unused commitment as expense on vendor invoice date unless contractual credits or rollovers exist.

Examples (practical scenarios)
----------------------------
1) Dedicated GPU pool reserved capacity (3-year commitment)
   - Approval: VP of Engineering -> Procurement -> Legal -> CFO.
   - Billing trigger: quarterly in advance per committed capacity.
   - Finance treatment: expense recognized when billed; related customer revenue recognized when we host customer workloads (ensure internal transfer pricing mirrors committed schedule).
2) Professional services (integration) tied to a customer implementation
   - Approval: Director Product -> Procurement -> Legal.
   - Billing trigger: milestone-based.
   - Finance treatment: vendor expenses recognized per milestone; Redwood invoices customer per product contract milestone — Product and Legal must ensure milestone definitions match.

Checklist for Requestors (minimum attachments)
--------------------------------------------
- Vendor quote or SOW
- Business justification and expected ROI or product impact
- Cost center and budget approver name
- Expected service commencement date and billing trigger recommendation
- Security questionnaire (if handling customer data)
- Any required export-control or compliance notes

Delegated Signature Authority (summary)
-------------------------------------
- Legal Counsel: up to $500k for standard SaaS templates (with no high-risk clauses)
- VP/Director of Finance: approve non-binding MSA addenda < $250k
- CFO: approves multiyear commitments > $250k and <= $2M
- CEO: approvals > $2M or any arrangement with strategic/board impact
Full authority matrix and delegation letters are in the Contracts Admin folder.

SLA Targets and Escalation Path
-------------------------------
- Standard legal review (non-complex): 7 business days.
- High-risk contracts (data residency, HSM, on-prem): 15 business days.
- If any stage exceeds SLA, Procurement will escalate to the approver’s manager and add an expedited tag. For > 10 business day delays in total, the Procurement Director performs a weekly review with stakeholder owners.

Common Pitfalls & Mitigations
----------------------------
- Mismatched milestones between vendor invoice schedule and customer billing: mitigation — require cross-signed milestone appendix and Finance sign-off pre-execution.
- Hidden termination or auto-renew clauses without notice periods: mitigation — Legal to enforce 30–90 day advance notice for renewals and include renewal reminders in contract registry.
- Security attachments missing: mitigation — block signature until Security & Compliance clears or issues a formal exception.

Operational Templates and Tools
------------------------------
- Procurement Portal: https://procurement.redwood.internal (internal) — use for all intake and auto-routing.
- Contract Registry spreadsheet / Confluence index (Contract-ID lookup) — upload signed agreements and set Billing Trigger field.
- Standard SOW and SaaS MSA templates are available in the Contracts library (link in related pages).

Change Log
----------
- 2025-11-12: Document created by Priya Natarajan (initial published).
- 2026-02-08: Updated approval thresholds and added committed capacity guidance for dedicated GPU pools; minor SLA adjustments.

Appendix: Example Billing Trigger JSON (for Contract Registry)
---------------------------------------------------------
Use the following example when filling the Billing Trigger field in the registry:

{
  "contract_id": "RWI-2026-0123",
  "billing_trigger": "quarterly_in_advance",
  "service_commencement": "2026-04-01",
  "revrec_note": "expense recognized on invoice; customer revenue recognized per usage"
}

Contact
-------
Procurement team (procurement@redwood.ai), Finance contracts (finance-contracts@redwood.ai), and Legal (legal-ops@redwood.ai). For urgent escalations, contact the Procurement Director (procurement-director@redwood.ai).

References
----------
- Capitalization policy (finance-and-legal/capitalization-policy-2025)
- Contract registry process (finance-and-legal/procurement/contract-registry-howto-2025)
- RevRec principles (finance-and-legal/revrec-and-billing/revrec-principles-2024)

- Any required export-control or compliance notes

Delegated Signature Authority (summary)
-------------------------------------
- Legal Counsel: up to $500k for standard SaaS templates (with no high-risk clauses)
- VP/Director of Finance: approve non-binding MSA addenda < $250k
- CFO: approves multiyear commitments > $250k and <= $2M
- CEO: approvals > $2M or any arrangement with strategic/board impact
Full authority matrix and delegation letters are in the Contracts Admin folder.

SLA Targets and Escalation Path
-------------------------------
- Standard legal review (non-complex): 7 business days.
- High-risk contracts (data residency, HSM, on-prem): 15 business days.
- If any stage exceeds SLA, Procurement will escalate to the approver’s manager and add an expedited tag. For > 10 business day delays in total, the Procurement Director performs a weekly review with stakeholder owners.

Common Pitfalls & Mitigations
----------------------------
- Mismatched milestones between vendor invoice schedule and customer billing: mitigation — require cross-signed milestone appendix and Finance sign-off pre-execution.
- Hidden termination or auto-renew clauses without notice periods: mitigation — Legal to enforce 30–90 day advance notice for renewals and include renewal reminders in contract registry.
- Security attachments missing: mitigation — block signature until Security & Compliance clears or issues a formal exception.

Operational Templates and Tools
------------------------------
- Procurement Portal: https://procurement.redwood.internal (internal) — use for all intake and auto-routing.
- Contract Registry spreadsheet / Confluence index (Contract-ID lookup) — upload signed agreements and set Billing Trigger field.
- Standard SOW and SaaS MSA templates are available in the Contracts library (link in related pages).

Change Log
----------
- 2025-11-12: Document created by Priya Natarajan (initial published).
- 2026-02-08: Updated approval thresholds and added committed capacity guidance for dedicated GPU pools; minor SLA adjustments.

Appendix: Example Billing Trigger JSON (for Contract Registry)
---------------------------------------------------------
Use the following example when filling the Billing Trigger field in the registry:

{
  "contract_id": "RWI-2026-0123",
  "billing_trigger": "quarterly_in_advance",
  "service_commencement": "2026-04-01",
  "revrec_note": "expense recognized on invoice; customer revenue recognized per usage"
}

Contact
-------
Procurement team (procurement@redwood.ai), Finance contracts (finance-contracts@redwood.ai), and Legal (legal-ops@redwood.ai). For urgent escalations, contact the Procurement Director (procurement-director@redwood.ai).

References
----------
- Capitalization policy (finance-and-legal/capitalization-policy-2025)
- Contract registry process (finance-and-legal/procurement/contract-registry-howto-2025)
- RevRec principles (finance-and-legal/revrec-and-billing/revrec-principles-2024)

source.title: Phased Invoicing and Commitment Optimization: Guidance for Contracts, Billing, and RevRec
source.author: Elena Torres
source.content:
Summary
-------
This document defines Redwood Inference's recommended approach for phased invoicing, proration, and revenue recognition for multi-period commitments (annual/multi-year) with variable usage and mixed billing components (committed capacity, overage, one-time fees, and credits). It is intended for Finance (RevOps), Legal, Sales, Procurement, and Billing engineering teams. The guidance balances predictable customer billing cadence with sound revrec accounting and clear operational procedures for reconciling committed usage and invoiced amounts.

Scope
-----
Applies to:
- New and renewed commercial contracts with any committed capacity or prepaid credits.
- Multi-year subscriptions, reserved/dedicated capacity contracts, and hybrid pricing (commit + usage).
Excludes: purely pay-as-you-go short-term trial invoices (covered in billing SOPs).

Definitions
-----------
- Commitment: customer contractual obligation for a minimum spend or reserved capacity (e.g., $X/month reserved GPU pool).
- Phased invoicing: splitting invoicing events across the commitment term (monthly/quarterly/annual invoices) with planned true-ups.
- True-up: reconciliation of committed vs actual usage and adjustment (credit/refund/invoice) at specified cadence.
- Deferred Revenue Trigger: event when cash is received but revenue is recognized later per ASC 606 (or applicable GAAP).

High-level Principles
---------------------
1. Predictability for customers: prefer monthly or quarterly invoices when operationally feasible for high-volume customers; annual prepayments are allowed but require clear true-up windows.
2. Accounting integrity: revenue recognition must follow ASC 606; recognize revenue when control of service is transferred and in proportion to satisfaction of performance obligations.
3. Minimal manual reconciliation: automate usage collection and invoice generation where possible; define clear tolerances for auto-credits.
4. Contract-first triggers: billing behavior must be driven by explicit contract clauses. If contract is silent, default to the standard company template (see Clause Templates section).

SLOs and Operational Targets
----------------------------
- Invoice issuance: 95% of recurring invoices issued within 5 business days of period start for prepay; 98% within 7 business days for postpay windows.
- True-up processing: 90% of monthly true-up adjustments posted within 10 business days of usage data availability.
- Dispute resolution: 80% of invoice disputes resolved within 15 business days after ticket creation.
- Reconciliation accuracy: monthly automated reconciliation should reconcile >= 99.5% of invoiceable usage; remainder escalated to manual review.

Contract Clauses (recommended language)
-------------------------------------
1) Billing Cadence & Proration (sample):
"Billing cadence will be monthly unless otherwise stated. Charges for the initial partial billing period will be prorated on a daily basis. If Customer prepays for a term, Redwood will invoice at the start of the term and perform a quarterly true-up against actual usage; any overage will be invoiced and any shortfall will be credited as described below."

2) True-up & Credits (sample):
"At each true-up interval (quarterly or annually), Redwood will compare committed units to actual units. If actual usage exceeds committed units, Customer will be invoiced for overage at the agreed rates. If actual usage is below committed units, Customer will receive a credit applied to future invoices. Credits are non-refundable unless otherwise required by law or contract amendment."

3) Early Termination / Refunds (sample):
"If Customer terminates without cause prior to the end of the prepaid term, Redwood will retain any earned, unbilled usage and may prorate refundable prepayments according to the schedule in Appendix A. Refund calculations exclude third-party pass-through charges and non-cancellable reserved resource costs incurred by Redwood."

Procedures: Pricing, Invoicing, and True-up
-----------------------------------------
A. Setup (Sales / Contracting)
1. Sales selects billing cadence in CLM and flags whether the commitment is: prepaid, monthly-billed, or hybrid.
2. Contract must include: commitment baseline units, unit pricing tiers, true-up cadence, invoicing currency, and refund/credit policy.
3. Legal verifies clause templates and attaches the billing appendix to the SOW.

B. Billing System Configuration (Billing Eng)
1. For each contract create: Billing_Profile (id), Commitment_Line_Items (CLI), Measurement_Window, TrueUp_Window (monthly/quarterly/annual), Prepay_Flag.
2. Attach GL mapping for deferred revenue, billed revenue, and credit memos.
3. Schedule automated jobs: usage-collection -> invoice-draft -> review -> finalization.

C. Monthly Flow (example sequence)
1. Day 1-3: Usage ingestion job finalizes the previous month's usage.
2. Day 4: Reconciliation job matches usage to commitment lines; generate Delta = actual - committed.
3. Day 5-7: Draft invoices and queued credits created. Finance review window (3 business days).
4. Day 8-10: Final invoices issued; credits applied to next invoice or issued as credit memo per contract.

D. Quarterly / Annual True-up
1. Aggregate the period; calculate net overage or underutilization.
2. If net overage > 2% of committed spend, auto-invoice for the full delta. If net underutilization > 5% of committed spend, auto-credit (note: thresholds meant to minimize churn from small variances).
3. Post true-up, Finance posts adjustments to GL: debit/credit Deferred Revenue and recognize Earned Revenue in the appropriate period.

Table: Example GL Mapping
| Bucket | GL Account (example) | Recognition treatment | Timing |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------------|--------|
| Prepaid commitment | 2200 Deferred Revenue - Commit | Deferred and amortized | Monthly over term |
| Overage invoice | 4001 Revenue - Overage | Recognize on invoice date or as performance satisfied | Immediate |
| Credit memo | 2205 Credit Memos | Reduce future invoices or refund | Applied on next invoice or refunded |

Reconciliation and Audit Trail
------------------------------
- Required data elements for automated reconciliation (CSV / parquet / event schema):
  - invoice_id, contract_id, measurement_window_start, measurement_window_end, committed_units, actual_units, unit_price, overage_amount, credit_amount, currency, time_zone
- Sample CSV header:
  invoice_id,contract_id,period_start,period_end,committed_units,actual_units,unit_price,overage_amount,credit_amount,currency
- Minimal SQL to detect reconciliation mismatches (billing schema table: billing_line_items):
  SELECT contract_id, period_start, period_end, SUM(billed_amount) as billed, SUM(usage_amount) as usage_charge
  FROM billing_line_items
  WHERE period_start >= '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY contract_id, period_start, period_end
  HAVING ABS(SUM(billed_amount) - SUM(usage_amount)) > 1.0;

Sample Invoice Event JSON (for billing-engine engineers)
{
  "invoice_id": "INV-2026-000123",
  "contract_id": "C-89012",
  "period_start": "2026-01-01",
  "period_end": "2026-01-31",
  "lines": [
    {"type": "commitment", "qty": "1000", "unit_price": "10.00", "amount": "10000.00"},
    {"type": "overage", "qty": "120", "unit_price": "12.00", "amount": "1440.00"},
    {"type": "credit", "qty": "-80", "unit_price": "10.00", "amount": "-800.00"}
  ],
  "total_due": "10840.00",
  "currency": "USD"
}

Handling Disputes and Adjustments
--------------------------------
1. Dispute intake: Customer Success creates a ticket with invoice_id and dispute_reason.
2. Finance triage: finance-rep assigns within 48 hours, confirms dispute window (must be raised within 60 days unless contract states otherwise).
3. If dispute requires usage reprocessing, Billing Eng re-runs ingestion for the impacted window and provides new draft within 5 business days.
4. If dispute is validated, create credit memo and mark invoice as adjusted in AR system.

Escalation Matrix
- Tier 1 (Billing Ops): initial triage and simple credits (< $5k). Owner: Billing Ops lead.
- Tier 2 (Finance): complex reconciliations > $5k or legal-impacting issues. Owner: Revenue Operations Manager.
- Tier 3 (Legal): contract interpretation, termination clauses, refunds > $50k. Owner: General Counsel.

Implementation Checklist for a New Commitment Contract
-----------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Sales marks contract type and billing cadence in CLM
- [ ] Legal confirms clause variants and finalizes SOW
- [ ] Billing Eng creates Billing_Profile and GL mapping
- [ ] Finance sets deferred revenue schedule and posts initial entries upon invoice
- [ ] Customer Success configures support playbook for dispute handling

Appendix A — Proration Examples
-------------------------------
1) Mid-month activation for monthly prepaid (example): Customer prepays $12,000 annual upfront for 12 monthly prepaid invoices. If activation date is 2026-01-10, first invoice covers 2026-01-10 to 2026-01-31 (22 days). Prorated amount = annual / 365 * 22 OR if contract uses monthly proration: (annual / 12) * (22/31) depending on clause.
2) Early termination refund example (rounded): Prepaid $12,000 for 12 months, used 90 days. Refund = prepaid - (earned by time) - non-refundable fees.

Appendix B — Example Contract Checklist Items to Include
--------------------------------------------------------
- Billing cadence and proration method (daily vs monthly)
- True-up cadence and threshold tolerances
- Credit vs refund policy
- Pass-through and third-party cost treatment
- Data retention / usage reporting SLA (e.g., usage data available within 48 hours of period end)
- Dispute window and escalation path

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Document owner: Finance - Revenue Operations (Elena Torres)
- Billing system owner: Billing Engineering (Jordan Blake)
- Legal lead (clauses & amendments): Sofia Martinez
- Sales escalation contact: Ravi Patel

Change Log
----------
- 2025-02-12: Created initial guidance (Elena Torres).
- 2025-09-03: Added SLOs and thresholds for automated reconciliation (Ravi Patel).
- 2026-01-18: Clarified quarterly true-up thresholds and added GL mapping table (Finance).

Related Documents
-----------------
- Commitment lifecycle and credit policy (see related_pages)
- Dedication capacity allocation and rev handoff playbook

Questions or feedback? Open a ticket in #finance-billing or email revenue-ops@redwood.ai

Handling Disputes and Adjustments
--------------------------------
1. Dispute intake: Customer Success creates a ticket with invoice_id and dispute_reason.
2. Finance triage: finance-rep assigns within 48 hours, confirms dispute window (must be raised within 60 days unless contract states otherwise).
3. If dispute requires usage reprocessing, Billing Eng re-runs ingestion for the impacted window and provides new draft within 5 business days.
4. If dispute is validated, create credit memo and mark invoice as adjusted in AR system.

Escalation Matrix
- Tier 1 (Billing Ops): initial triage and simple credits (< $5k). Owner: Billing Ops lead.
- Tier 2 (Finance): complex reconciliations > $5k or legal-impacting issues. Owner: Revenue Operations Manager.
- Tier 3 (Legal): contract interpretation, termination clauses, refunds > $50k. Owner: General Counsel.

Implementation Checklist for a New Commitment Contract
-----------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Sales marks contract type and billing cadence in CLM
- [ ] Legal confirms clause variants and finalizes SOW
- [ ] Billing Eng creates Billing_Profile and GL mapping
- [ ] Finance sets deferred revenue schedule and posts initial entries upon invoice
- [ ] Customer Success configures support playbook for dispute handling

Appendix A — Proration Examples
-------------------------------
1) Mid-month activation for monthly prepaid (example): Customer prepays $12,000 annual upfront for 12 monthly prepaid invoices. If activation date is 2026-01-10, first invoice covers 2026-01-10 to 2026-01-31 (22 days). Prorated amount = annual / 365 * 22 OR if contract uses monthly proration: (annual / 12) * (22/31) depending on clause.
2) Early termination refund example (rounded): Prepaid $12,000 for 12 months, used 90 days. Refund = prepaid - (earned by time) - non-refundable fees.

Appendix B — Example Contract Checklist Items to Include
--------------------------------------------------------
- Billing cadence and proration method (daily vs monthly)
- True-up cadence and threshold tolerances
- Credit vs refund policy
- Pass-through and third-party cost treatment
- Data retention / usage reporting SLA (e.g., usage data available within 48 hours of period end)
- Dispute window and escalation path

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Document owner: Finance - Revenue Operations (Elena Torres)
- Billing system owner: Billing Engineering (Jordan Blake)
- Legal lead (clauses & amendments): Sofia Martinez
- Sales escalation contact: Ravi Patel

Change Log
----------
- 2025-02-12: Created initial guidance (Elena Torres).
- 2025-09-03: Added SLOs and thresholds for automated reconciliation (Ravi Patel).
- 2026-01-18: Clarified quarterly true-up thresholds and added GL mapping table (Finance).

Related Documents
-----------------
- Commitment lifecycle and credit policy (see related_pages)
- Dedication capacity allocation and rev handoff playbook

Questions or feedback? Open a ticket in #finance-billing or email revenue-ops@redwood.ai

source.title: Blended Cost Methodology v1 (Unit Economics Dashboard)
source.author: Rishi Malhotra
source.content:
## Purpose
This document defines the Finance-aligned methodology for computing **Blended Cost per 1M Output Tokens** (“blended $/1M output tokens”) as used in Redwood’s internal **Unit Economics Dashboard**. It is intended to:
- Provide a consistent metric definition that reconciles (within agreed tolerances) to Finance reporting and infrastructure spend inputs.
- Establish the source of truth for the GPU cost rate card and its update/versioning cadence.
- Specify allocation rules (including shared overhead and idle capacity treatment) so cross-team analysis uses the same assumptions.
- Define required reconciliation checks and sign-off gates for executive reporting.

This is **v1** of the methodology. Changes must follow the versioning process described below.

## Metric definition (v1)
### Primary KPI
**Blended Cost per 1M Output Tokens**

**Formula:**
- **Blended $/1M output tokens** = (Allocated cost in USD / Total output tokens) * 1,000,000

**Unit:** USD per 1,000,000 output tokens.

### What counts as “output tokens”
Included:
- Tokens generated by the model and returned to the caller in both streaming and non-streaming responses.
- Tokens generated for tool/function-call structured outputs when they are part of the model’s generated output.

Excluded:
- Input/prompt tokens.
- Tokens generated internally but not returned due to request cancellation, truncation before delivery, or transport errors (unless explicitly flagged as delivered; see “Known limitations”).

Token measurement source:
- Platform metering pipeline (token counters emitted by gateway/runtime) aggregated hourly.

### Scope of “allocated cost”
Included costs (v1):
- GPU compute cost applied via standardized hourly **GPU rate card** (provider/region/capacity type).
- Approved, explicitly enumerated “serving overhead” adders applied as a percentage of GPU compute (see Overhead policy).

Excluded costs (v1):
- Corporate overhead not attributable to serving (e.g., G&A).
- R&D/training spend.
- One-time incident costs unless explicitly incorporated into the rate card for the month/quarter.

## Segmentation and rollups
The dashboard supports drilldowns by:
- **Model** (and version / quantization profile)
- **Region** (normalized canonical region)
- **Customer tier** (self-serve / dedicated / private / enterprise)

Finance reporting commonly requires rollups:
- Company-wide (all tiers)
- Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private separation
- Region rollup (e.g., NA/EU) for exec narratives

**Important:** The KPI is reported as a blended measure, not a marginal cost. Shifts in traffic mix, idle allocation, and rate card changes can move the KPI even if runtime efficiency is flat.

## Data sources (sources of truth)
### A) Token denominators
**Source:** Token metering pipeline aggregates (hourly)
- Required dimensions (v1): model_id, model_version (or equivalent canonical), region_canonical, customer_tier, streaming_flag, quantization_profile.
- Completeness requirements are governed by the Unit Economics Pipeline SLO page.

### B) GPU cost inputs
**Source:** Finance GPU cost rate card (versioned)
- The rate card provides an hourly USD rate by:
  - cloud/provider
  - region
  - GPU type (where applicable)
  - capacity type (on-demand vs reserved/committed vs dedicated pool)

**Provenance:** The rate card is derived from a combination of invoices, cloud billing exports, and committed contract schedules.

### C) Capacity accounting / utilization
**Source:** Infra capacity telemetry + scheduler utilization reports
- Used to estimate how many GPU-hours to attribute to each segment.
- Utilization is used for allocation of shared pools and idle capacity treatment.

### D) Overhead adders
**Source:** Finance-approved overhead policy (v1)
- Overhead adders are used only where direct invoiced attribution is not available or would be unstable at hourly granularity.

## Allocation model (v1)
The allocation model assigns GPU-hours and cost to segments (model/region/tier) using token-weighted and pool-aware logic.

### 1) Cost pools
Costs are grouped into pools prior to allocation:
- **Hosted shared pools** (multi-tenant GPU fleets)
- **Dedicated pools** (customer-reserved GPU pools)
- **Private deployments** (customer VPC/on-prem; cost model differs)

Each pool has its own allocation rule.

### 2) Allocation basis by pool
#### Hosted shared pools
- Allocation basis: **output tokens by segment** within the pool, within the hour.
- If a segment is missing required dimensions (e.g., region unknown), it is allocated to “unknown” and excluded from segment drilldowns until remediated. Finance rollups must include “unknown” to avoid undercounting.

#### Dedicated pools
- Allocation basis: **contracted capacity** first, then actual utilization where available.
- v1 approach:
  - If dedicated GPU-hours are explicitly tracked: allocate direct GPU-hours to that customer tier/region, then distribute to models by output token share within that customer’s traffic.
  - If not explicitly tracked: allocate cost based on reserved capacity hours (contract schedule) regardless of utilization; see Idle capacity treatment.

#### Private deployments
- v1 dashboard reporting:
  - Where Redwood bears infrastructure cost (managed private): treat as a separate pool and allocate similarly to Dedicated.
  - Where customer bears infrastructure cost (on-prem): **excluded from GPU cost numerator**; output tokens may be present for product analytics, but Finance KPI reporting should exclude these rows to avoid distorting company blended cost.

### 3) Idle capacity treatment
Idle capacity is a first-order driver of blended cost and must be consistent with Finance.

v1 policy:
- **Hosted shared pools:** idle GPU-hours are allocated across segments in the pool proportional to output tokens for the hour (i.e., idle cost is blended into unit cost).
- **Dedicated pools:** idle cost is treated as part of the customer’s pool cost (reflecting the reserved nature of the contract). If traffic is low, unit cost increases; this is expected.

Rationale:
- This aligns with how Finance interprets committed capacity costs: the cost exists regardless of utilization.

### 4) Overhead policy (v1)
Overhead is applied as an adder on GPU compute cost for each pool.

v1 overhead adders:
- Hosted shared pools: +X% (Finance-controlled value set per quarter)
- Dedicated pools: +Y% (may differ due to support/ops overhead)
- Private managed: +Z%

Note: Specific percentages are maintained in the rate card version notes and are not duplicated here to avoid stale documentation.

## Rate card management
### Rate card update cadence
- **Monthly refresh** for invoice/billing alignment.
- **Quarterly lock** for executive KPI decks and board reporting.

### Versioning rules
- Each rate card release is versioned (e.g., 2025Q4, 2026Q1).
- The Unit Economics Dashboard must:
  - Persist the rate card version used for any aggregate.
  - Allow Finance to rerun historical periods using the version in effect at the time (reporting stability).

### Effective dates
- Monthly rate updates are effective at the start of the month in **UTC**, unless otherwise specified in the release note.
- Finance reporting may use month boundaries in Pacific Time for close processes; where required, Finance will specify a “reporting calendar” mapping. Default dashboard time boundaries remain UTC.

## Reconciliation methodology
Finance requires that the KPI is defensible and reconcilable to spend inputs and platform metering.

### Reconciliation checkpoints (v1)
1) **Token denominator reconciliation**
   - Compare aggregated output tokens from the unit economics tables vs the canonical metering totals for the same window.
   - Expected variance: <= 0.5% for stable periods; investigate if > 1%.

2) **Cost numerator reconciliation**
   - Compare allocated GPU cost totals vs cost pool totals from the rate card * GPU-hours inputs.
   - Expected variance: 0% after deterministic allocation (any difference indicates missing rows or join/filter bugs).

3) **Spend-to-billing sanity check (monthly)**
   - Compare total allocated compute cost vs cloud billing exports/invoices for the month.
   - Tolerance: Finance-defined; typically within 2–5% depending on invoice timing, credits, and accrual estimates.

4) **Customer-tier split reasonableness**
   - Validate that Hosted/Dedicated/Private pool totals align with internal tagging and contract schedules.
   - Spot check a sample of known Dedicated customers monthly.

5) **Outlier review**
   - Top-N segments by unit cost and by token volume.
   - Verify that spikes correspond to known events (rate changes, utilization drops, region incidents, model rollouts).

### Required artifacts for Finance sign-off
For each monthly/quarterly reporting cycle:
- Rate card version and change log
- Reconciliation report output (tokens + cost + variances)
- List of known data quality incidents affecting the window (with impact estimate)

## Treatment of credits, discounts, and revenue
This dashboard metric is **cost-based**. It is not directly netted against:
- customer credits
- pricing discounts
- promotional programs

FP&A may separately compute contribution margin metrics that incorporate revenue. Do not mix definitions in executive narratives without explicit labeling.

## Data quality requirements (Finance view)
The KPI is considered Finance-reportable only if:
- Data freshness SLO is met for the reporting window.
- Dimension completeness meets thresholds (region/model/tier not missing beyond threshold).
- Streaming and non-streaming token parity checks pass.

When these gates fail, the dashboard must either:
- show a “data confidence” warning and block export to KPI pack, or
- require an explicit Finance override with a recorded reason.

## Operational cadence and ownership
- Methodology owner: **FP&A (Rishi Malhotra)**
- Finance sponsor: **CFO (Laura Bennett)**
- Engineering owners:
  - Token metering correctness: **Nadia Rahman / Telemetry & Analytics**
  - Aggregation job + warehouse views: **Logan Wright / Platform Engineering**
  - Dashboard surfaces: **Console & Tooling**

Monthly process:
1) Finance publishes rate card update (if applicable)
2) Engineering validates ingestion of new rate card version
3) Reconciliation report run and reviewed
4) Finance sign-off recorded; KPI pack sources updated

## Known limitations (v1)
- Streaming delivery edge cases: If the client disconnects mid-stream, “generated” vs “delivered” token semantics may diverge depending on runtime path. v1 uses “delivered” where instrumented; otherwise defaults to runtime counter. This is tracked as an instrumentation gap.
- Private/on-prem cost exclusion: On-prem deployments where the customer bears infra cost are excluded from the cost numerator; tokens may still appear for operational visibility.
- Timezone boundary mismatches: Dashboard defaults to UTC hourly rollups; Finance month-close may require alternate boundaries.

## Change control
Any changes to:
- inclusion/exclusion rules
- idle capacity treatment
- overhead adders
- rate card provenance or versioning

must be documented as a methodology update and, where material, accompanied by an ADR and a backtest showing impact on prior periods.

## Appendix A: Glossary
- **Output tokens:** Tokens returned to the caller as part of model output.
- **Blended cost:** Average cost including idle and shared overhead allocations.
- **Pool:** A grouping of capacity/cost that shares an allocation rule (hosted shared, dedicated, private).
- **Rate card:** Finance-maintained table mapping capacity inputs to hourly USD cost assumptions.

## Appendix B: Reporting guidance (do’s and don’ts)
Do:
- Always label the metric as **blended** and specify the time window and rate card version.
- Use drilldowns to identify cost drivers (region mix, quant profile changes, caching/batching changes).

Don’t:
- Compare two windows without checking for rate card version changes.
- Interpret a unit cost move as purely runtime regression without verifying utilization and traffic mix.

- v1 dashboard reporting:
  - Where Redwood bears infrastructure cost (managed private): treat as a separate pool and allocate similarly to Dedicated.
  - Where customer bears infrastructure cost (on-prem): **excluded from GPU cost numerator**; output tokens may be present for product analytics, but Finance KPI reporting should exclude these rows to avoid distorting company blended cost.

### 3) Idle capacity treatment
Idle capacity is a first-order driver of blended cost and must be consistent with Finance.

v1 policy:
- **Hosted shared pools:** idle GPU-hours are allocated across segments in the pool proportional to output tokens for the hour (i.e., idle cost is blended into unit cost).
- **Dedicated pools:** idle cost is treated as part of the customer’s pool cost (reflecting the reserved nature of the contract). If traffic is low, unit cost increases; this is expected.

Rationale:
- This aligns with how Finance interprets committed capacity costs: the cost exists regardless of utilization.

### 4) Overhead policy (v1)
Overhead is applied as an adder on GPU compute cost for each pool.

v1 overhead adders:
- Hosted shared pools: +X% (Finance-controlled value set per quarter)
- Dedicated pools: +Y% (may differ due to support/ops overhead)
- Private managed: +Z%

Note: Specific percentages are maintained in the rate card version notes and are not duplicated here to avoid stale documentation.

## Rate card management
### Rate card update cadence
- **Monthly refresh** for invoice/billing alignment.
- **Quarterly lock** for executive KPI decks and board reporting.

### Versioning rules
- Each rate card release is versioned (e.g., 2025Q4, 2026Q1).
- The Unit Economics Dashboard must:
  - Persist the rate card version used for any aggregate.
  - Allow Finance to rerun historical periods using the version in effect at the time (reporting stability).

### Effective dates
- Monthly rate updates are effective at the start of the month in **UTC**, unless otherwise specified in the release note.
- Finance reporting may use month boundaries in Pacific Time for close processes; where required, Finance will specify a “reporting calendar” mapping. Default dashboard time boundaries remain UTC.

## Reconciliation methodology
Finance requires that the KPI is defensible and reconcilable to spend inputs and platform metering.

### Reconciliation checkpoints (v1)
1) **Token denominator reconciliation**
   - Compare aggregated output tokens from the unit economics tables vs the canonical metering totals for the same window.
   - Expected variance: <= 0.5% for stable periods; investigate if > 1%.

2) **Cost numerator reconciliation**
   - Compare allocated GPU cost totals vs cost pool totals from the rate card * GPU-hours inputs.
   - Expected variance: 0% after deterministic allocation (any difference indicates missing rows or join/filter bugs).

3) **Spend-to-billing sanity check (monthly)**
   - Compare total allocated compute cost vs cloud billing exports/invoices for the month.
   - Tolerance: Finance-defined; typically within 2–5% depending on invoice timing, credits, and accrual estimates.

4) **Customer-tier split reasonableness**
   - Validate that Hosted/Dedicated/Private pool totals align with internal tagging and contract schedules.
   - Spot check a sample of known Dedicated customers monthly.

5) **Outlier review**
   - Top-N segments by unit cost and by token volume.
   - Verify that spikes correspond to known events (rate changes, utilization drops, region incidents, model rollouts).

### Required artifacts for Finance sign-off
For each monthly/quarterly reporting cycle:
- Rate card version and change log
- Reconciliation report output (tokens + cost + variances)
- List of known data quality incidents affecting the window (with impact estimate)

## Treatment of credits, discounts, and revenue
This dashboard metric is **cost-based**. It is not directly netted against:
- customer credits
- pricing discounts
- promotional programs

FP&A may separately compute contribution margin metrics that incorporate revenue. Do not mix definitions in executive narratives without explicit labeling.

## Data quality requirements (Finance view)
The KPI is considered Finance-reportable only if:
- Data freshness SLO is met for the reporting window.
- Dimension completeness meets thresholds (region/model/tier not missing beyond threshold).
- Streaming and non-streaming token parity checks pass.

When these gates fail, the dashboard must either:
- show a “data confidence” warning and block export to KPI pack, or
- require an explicit Finance override with a recorded reason.

4) Finance sign-off recorded; KPI pack sources updated

## Known limitations (v1)
- Streaming delivery edge cases: If the client disconnects mid-stream, “generated” vs “delivered” token semantics may diverge depending on runtime path. v1 uses “delivered” where instrumented; otherwise defaults to runtime counter. This is tracked as an instrumentation gap.
- Private/on-prem cost exclusion: On-prem deployments where the customer bears infra cost are excluded from the cost numerator; tokens may still appear for operational visibility.
- Timezone boundary mismatches: Dashboard defaults to UTC hourly rollups; Finance month-close may require alternate boundaries.

## Change control
Any changes to:
- inclusion/exclusion rules
- idle capacity treatment
- overhead adders
- rate card provenance or versioning

must be documented as a methodology update and, where material, accompanied by an ADR and a backtest showing impact on prior periods.

## Appendix A: Glossary
- **Output tokens:** Tokens returned to the caller as part of model output.
- **Blended cost:** Average cost including idle and shared overhead allocations.
- **Pool:** A grouping of capacity/cost that shares an allocation rule (hosted shared, dedicated, private).
- **Rate card:** Finance-maintained table mapping capacity inputs to hourly USD cost assumptions.

## Appendix B: Reporting guidance (do’s and don’ts)
Do:
- Always label the metric as **blended** and specify the time window and rate card version.
- Use drilldowns to identify cost drivers (region mix, quant profile changes, caching/batching changes).

Don’t:
- Compare two windows without checking for rate card version changes.
- Interpret a unit cost move as purely runtime regression without verifying utilization and traffic mix.
